Simple meaning

At every cycle, the agent should choose the action that removes the most important uncertainty.

Why it matters

Poor action choice creates unnecessary tool calls, cost, latency, and risk.

Weak prompt

Call any tools that may be useful.

Strong prompt

Choose only one next action. It must resolve the highest-priority missing fact and be justified by current state.

Business example
Current state:
Payment successful
Order dispatched
Courier status unknown

Available tools:
get_payment_status
get_inventory
get_courier_status
create_refund

Best next action:
get_courier_status

Common mistakes

  • Calling all tools.
  • Rechecking known information.
  • Selecting a write action before investigation.
  • Choosing an expensive tool too early.
Mini exercise

Order is known, but tracking details are missing. What should the agent do?

Answer: Retrieve the tracking or courier information.

Memory line

The next action should remove the biggest blocker, not create more activity.

Simple meaning

A ReAct cycle usually chooses one main action before reassessing.

Why it matters

This prevents later actions from executing on assumptions that may become invalid.

Weak prompt

Call order, courier, refund, policy, and email tools together.

Strong prompt

Call the next required tool only. After observing its result, decide whether the remaining actions are still valid.

Example
Bad:
Check courier
Create replacement
Send email
—all before courier result is known

Better:
Check courier
→ observe package lost
→ check policy
→ observe replacement allowed
→ create replacement

When parallel actions are acceptable

Independent read-only calls may run together, such as inventory, supplier lead time, and open purchase orders.

Common mistake

Executing a chain based on assumptions rather than observations.

Memory line

Act, observe, then decide again.

Simple meaning

An observation must be valid before it updates state.

Why it matters

A tool may return the wrong entity, stale data, partial data, invalid schema, or untrusted text.

Weak prompt

Use whatever the tool returned.

Strong prompt

Validate the observation by checking the entity, schema, freshness, completeness, and source authority before updating state.

Example
Requested:
Order 2056

Returned:
Order 3021

Correct response:
Reject observation
Do not update state

Common mistakes

  • HTTP success treated as business success.
  • Wrong identifier ignored.
  • Old result accepted.
  • Tool output treated as instruction.
Mini exercise

A courier result has no timestamp. Can freshness be assumed?

Answer: No.

Memory line

ReAct is only as reliable as the observations it accepts.

Simple meaning

Each observation can create a different next action.

Weak prompt

Always create a replacement when delivery is late.

Strong prompt

Choose the next action by courier status: package_lost → retrieve replacement policy; invalid_address → request corrected address; delivered → verify proof of delivery; unknown → retry or escalate.

Customer-support example
Observation:
invalid_address

Correct next action:
Ask customer to confirm the address

Incorrect:
Create refund immediately

Common mistakes

  • No fallback for unknown status.
  • Old branch continues after new observation.
  • One branch used for every outcome.
Memory line

In ReAct, observations decide the path.

Simple meaning

Replanning changes the route when observations invalidate the current plan.

Why it matters

The original plan may become unnecessary, impossible, or unsafe.

Weak prompt

Continue the original plan regardless of results.

Strong prompt

After every material observation, check whether the plan is still valid, cancel obsolete tasks, add new required tasks, and record why the plan changed.

Example
Initial plan:
Check courier
Create replacement
Notify customer

Observation:
Order was never paid

Revised plan:
Cancel courier and replacement tasks
Start payment-recovery flow

Common mistakes

  • Obsolete tasks remain active.
  • Silent plan changes.
  • Replanning after every minor detail.
  • No link between observation and plan update.
Mini exercise

Approval is denied. What should ReAct do?

Answer: Replan and choose another allowed resolution.

Memory line

ReAct updates the plan using evidence instead of following it blindly.

Simple meaning

A tool error is an observation that should influence the next decision.

Why it matters

Errors should not automatically create endless retries.

Weak prompt

Keep retrying until it works.

Strong prompt

Retry temporary errors within limit, correct invalid arguments, escalate permission errors, use approved fallback when available, and stop when the budget is exhausted.

Example
Attempt 1 → timeout
Attempt 2 → timeout
Fallback endpoint → success

Common mistakes

  • Retrying permanent failures.
  • Infinite loops.
  • Same invalid argument reused.
  • Write action retried without idempotency.
Memory line

Tool failure is information; use it to choose recovery, not blind repetition.

Simple meaning

An approval result becomes part of the ReAct loop.

Why it matters

The agent must pause when authority is missing.

Weak prompt

Request approval and continue the refund.

Strong prompt

Treat approval as a blocking dependency. Do not execute the high-impact action until approval is verified as approved.

Finance example
Refund amount: ₹25,000
Agent limit: ₹10,000
Observation: approval pending
State: waiting_for_approval

Common mistakes

  • Approval request treated as approval.
  • Silence treated as consent.
  • Approval checked after action.
  • Expired approval reused.
Mini exercise

Approval status is pending. Can the refund execute?

Answer: No.

Memory line

In ReAct, approval is an observation that can block execution.

Simple meaning

The loop needs explicit stopping rules.

Why it matters

Without them, the agent may continue calling tools and consuming resources indefinitely.

Good stopping conditions

  • Goal criteria are satisfied.
  • No useful next action exists.
  • Required information is unavailable.
  • Approval is pending.
  • Retry budget is exhausted.
  • Human intervention is required.
  • Cost or time budget is reached.
Weak prompt

Continue until everything is perfect.

Strong prompt

Stop when the goal is verified complete, the next action cannot progress safely, human input is required, or retry/cost budget is exhausted.

Example
Replacement created: Yes
Customer notified: Yes
No unresolved task: Yes

Stop:
Goal complete

Common mistakes

  • No stop rule.
  • Tool availability treated as reason to continue.
  • Style improvement treated as unfinished work.
  • Repeated verification of known facts.
Mini exercise

Manager approval has not arrived. Keep polling continuously?

Answer: No. Stop or wait according to workflow policy.

Memory line

Every ReAct loop needs a reason to continue and a reason to stop.

Simple meaning

A loop occurs when the same action or reasoning repeats without meaningful state change.

Warning signs

Same tool repeated
Same result returned
No state change
Same question reconsidered
No progress toward criteria
Retry budget ignored
Weak prompt

Try again if unsure.

Strong prompt

Before repeating an action, confirm that the input changed, the failure is retryable, retry budget remains, and a different result is realistic.

Example
get_order_status("2056") → dispatched
get_order_status("2056") → dispatched
get_order_status("2056") → dispatched

No progress is being made.

Common solutions

  • Record completed actions.
  • Track attempts.
  • Compare old and new state.
  • Use retry limits.
  • Change strategy or escalate.
Mini exercise

The same tool returned the same result three times. What next?

Answer: Stop repeating and choose another action or escalate.

Memory line

Repetition without state change is not reasoning progress.

Simple meaning

A ReAct trace records the reason, action, observation, state change, and stop/continue decision.

Why it matters

It supports debugging, auditing, cost analysis, safety review, and loop detection.

Practical trace
Cycle 1
Reason: Order status missing
Action: get_order_status
Observation: dispatched, tracking_id=TRK9087
State change: tracking_id stored

Cycle 2
Reason: Courier status missing
Action: get_courier_status
Observation: package_lost
State change: shipment_issue=package_lost
Weak logging

Agent did some checks.

Strong logging

Record cycle number, action reason, tool name, sanitized arguments, observation, state change, cost, duration, and stop/continue decision.

Common mistake

Logging secrets or full customer data.

Memory line

A good ReAct trace explains how each observation changed the next action.

Simple meaning

ReAct does not require a long visible essay before every action.

Why it matters

Verbose reasoning increases tokens, latency, and unnecessary exposure of internal detail.

Weak design

Generate a long essay before every tool call.

Strong design

Store a short action justification, execute the tool, and record the resulting state change.

Compact trace
Missing: courier status
Action: get_courier_status
Result: package_lost
Next: search replacement policy

Common mistake

Confusing reasoning quality with reasoning length.

Memory line

ReAct needs clear decisions, not long essays.

Simple meaning

ReAct adapts dynamically. A fixed workflow follows predefined steps.

ReActFixed workflow
Dynamic next actionPredefined steps
Useful under uncertaintyUseful for stable processes
Model selects pathRules control path
More flexibleMore predictable
Higher control burdenEasier to test
Weak design

Use an autonomous ReAct loop for every business process.

Strong design

Use deterministic workflow for stable high-risk execution. Use ReAct only where interpretation, uncertainty, or dynamic tool selection is required.

Example
Use ReAct for:
Investigate why the shipment failed

Use workflow for:
Process an already approved refund

Common mistake

Replacing a safe deterministic workflow with an open-ended agent loop.

Memory line

Use ReAct for uncertainty and workflows for predictable control.

Simple meaning

Plan-and-execute defines more of the route in advance; ReAct decides incrementally.

Plan-and-executeReAct
Plan firstDecide one step at a time
Good for known structureGood for uncertain environments
Easier progress trackingMore adaptive
May need replanningNaturally observation-driven
Practical hybrid
Create high-level plan
    ↓
Use ReAct inside uncertain tasks
    ↓
Update plan after observations
Example
High-level plan:
Investigate
Select resolution
Execute
Notify

ReAct inside investigation:
Check order
→ check courier
→ retrieve policy

Common mistake

Treating plan-and-execute and ReAct as mutually exclusive.

Memory line

Plan the structure; use ReAct where the path is uncertain.

Cycle 1
Reason:
Order details are missing

Act:
get_order_status("2056")

Observe:
dispatched, tracking_id=TRK9087

Update:
order_status=dispatched
tracking_id=TRK9087
Cycle 2
Reason:
Courier status is missing

Act:
get_courier_status("TRK9087")

Observe:
package_lost

Update:
shipment_issue=package_lost
Cycle 3
Reason:
Current policy is required

Act:
search_replacement_policy("package_lost")

Observe:
replacement allowed
approval not required

Update:
replacement_eligible=true
Cycle 4
Reason:
Replacement is allowed and preferred

Act:
create_replacement(
  order_id="2056",
  idempotency_key="order2056-replacement-v1"
)

Observe:
replacement_id=RP88
status=success
Cycle 5
Reason:
Customer notification remains incomplete

Act:
create_customer_email_draft("2056")

Observe:
draft created
Stop check
Cause verified: Yes
Resolution executed: Yes
Customer notification completed: Yes
Open critical tasks: No

Goal complete
User Goal
    ↓
State Reader
    ↓
Reasoning Controller
    ↓
Next-Action Selector
    ↓
Tool and Permission Validator
    ↓
Action Executor
    ↓
Observation Validator
    ↓
State Updater
    ↓
Stop / Continue / Replan
while not goal_complete(goal, state):

    missing_fact = identify_highest_priority_gap(
        goal=goal,
        state=state
    )

    action = choose_next_action(
        missing_fact=missing_fact,
        tools=available_tools,
        constraints=constraints
    )

    validate_action(action)

    observation = execute(action)
    validate_observation(observation)

    update_state(state, observation)

    if should_replan(state, observation):
        revise_plan(state)

    if should_stop(state):
        break
AreaQuestion
GoalIs the desired outcome clear?
StateWhat is already known?
GapWhat is the most important missing fact?
ActionWhich one action can reduce that gap?
ToolIs the selected tool valid and allowed?
ObservationIs the result trustworthy and fresh?
UpdateWhat state changed?
BranchDoes the result change the path?
RetryIs failure retryable and within budget?
ApprovalIs execution blocked by human authorization?
LoopIs the same action repeating without progress?
StopAre success or stopping conditions satisfied?
Reason → Act → Observe → Update → Continue, Replan, or Stop
FocusFind one important missing fact.
ActionChoose one justified tool or step.
LearningValidate observation and update state.
ControlStop, replan, retry, or escalate safely.
Final memory line

ReAct is disciplined interaction with the environment: decide, act, observe, update, and stop when the goal is truly complete.

Quick knowledge check

1. What does ReAct mean?
Reason, act, observe, and repeat.
2. Why is ReAct useful?
Tool results can determine the next action.
3. What should happen after an observation?
Validate it and update state.
4. How should the next action be selected?
Choose the action that reduces the most important uncertainty.
5. Should every task use ReAct?
No. Simple deterministic tasks may not need it.
6. Why are stopping conditions important?
They prevent endless loops and unnecessary tool calls.
7. What follows a non-retryable error?
Escalate or choose another path instead of blindly retrying.
8. Can approval pending be treated as approved?
No.
9. ReAct versus workflow?
ReAct chooses dynamically; workflows follow predefined steps.
10. Safest ReAct principle?
Take one justified action, learn from the verified result, and continue only when progress remains.

Workflows provide predictable control. Agents provide adaptive decision-making. The strongest enterprise systems combine both deliberately.

Use workflows where the process is known. Use autonomy only where uncertainty genuinely requires it.
InterpretValidateExecuteVerify

Simple meaning

A deterministic workflow follows predefined steps and rules. The same input normally follows the same path.

Why it matters

Workflows are easier to test, audit, secure, monitor, explain, and control.

Detailed explanation

Approved request→Validate→Check records→Execute→Notify
Weak prompt

Handle the refund however you think is best.

Strong prompt

Follow the approved refund workflow: validate the request, confirm payment, check approval, create the refund, update the order, and notify the customer.

When to use

  • Steps are stable.
  • Rules are clear.
  • Compliance matters.
  • High-impact actions are involved.

When not to use

Do not force a fixed workflow when the problem is ambiguous or the route cannot be known in advance.

Business example

Processing an already approved refund is a workflow. Investigating why the refund failed may require an agent.

Common mistakes

  • Using an LLM for simple routing.
  • Hiding rules only inside prompts.
  • Allowing the model to reorder mandatory steps.
Mini exercise

Which fits a workflow better: churn investigation or a manager-approved expense claim?

Answer: The approved expense claim.

Memory line

A workflow is best when the route is already known.

Simple meaning

An autonomous agent decides what to do next based on the goal, state, observations, and available tools.

Why it matters

Some tasks require interpretation, investigation, replanning, comparison, or user clarification.

Weak prompt

Do anything necessary to solve the problem.

Strong prompt

Investigate the delay using approved read-only tools. Select one justified next action at a time and do not perform refunds or replacements without policy validation.

Developer example
Goal: Diagnose deployment failure

Possible agent actions:
- Read logs
- Inspect configuration
- Compare recent changes
- Run tests
- Propose correction

When to use

  • The path is uncertain.
  • Evidence must be gathered dynamically.
  • Several tools may apply.
  • New information may change the plan.

When not to use

  • Exact financial execution
  • Stable compliance processes
  • Simple calculations
  • Known data transformations

Common mistakes

  • Unrestricted authority.
  • No tool boundaries.
  • No stop condition.
  • No audit trace.
Mini exercise

Which benefits more from an agent: fixed tax calculation or unexplained production incident?

Answer: The production incident.

Memory line

An agent is useful when the route must be discovered during execution.

Simple meaning

A workflow follows a known route. An agent chooses the route.

AreaWorkflowAgent
PathPredefinedDynamic
Decision-makingRulesReasoning plus rules
PredictabilityHighLower
FlexibilityLowerHigher
TestingEasierHarder
Risk controlEasierNeeds stronger controls
Weak prompt

Use an agent because agents are more advanced.

Strong prompt

Choose a workflow when steps are stable and known. Use an agent only for uncertain parts requiring interpretation or dynamic decisions.

Example
Investigate a failed refund → Agent
Execute approved refund → Workflow

Common mistake

Assuming more autonomy always means a better system.

Memory line

More autonomy can mean more risk, not necessarily more value.

Simple meaning

Rule-based routing sends a request to a known workflow using explicit conditions.

Weak prompt

Ask the LLM to decide every route.

Strong prompt

Use deterministic routing for known request types and model-based routing only when the request is ambiguous.

Business example
Order status → Order workflow
Password reset → Identity workflow
Refund request → Refund workflow
Unknown complaint → Triage agent

When to use

  • Categories are known.
  • Inputs are structured.
  • The mapping is stable.

When not to use

Rules are less useful when requests are vague or contain multiple intents.

Common mistakes

  • LLM used for exact routing tables.
  • No fallback.
  • Rules not versioned.
Mini exercise

A request explicitly says “Check refund status.” Should an LLM decide the route?

Answer: Usually no.

Memory line

Use rules for known routes and reasoning for uncertain interpretation.

Simple meaning

Model-based routing uses an LLM to classify ambiguous language and select an approved destination.

Weak prompt

Choose any tool that seems related.

Strong prompt

Classify the request into one approved category and return category, confidence, required workflow, and whether clarification is needed.

Example
User:
My money has not come back yet.

Classification:
{
  "category":"refund_status",
  "confidence":0.91,
  "workflow":"refund_status_lookup"
}

When to use

  • Language is ambiguous.
  • Users use informal phrasing.
  • Multiple intents may exist.

When not to use

Do not use it when a structured field already provides the route.

Common mistakes

  • No confidence threshold.
  • No fallback.
  • Invented workflow names.
  • High-risk route not validated.
Mini exercise

“My package came, but one item is missing.” Likely category?

Answer: Missing-item or partial-delivery complaint.

Memory line

Model-based routing interprets language; trusted code validates the route.

Simple meaning

A hybrid system combines agent reasoning with deterministic workflows.

Why it matters

It gives flexibility where needed and control where required.

User request→Agent investigates→Structured decision→Workflow executes
Weak design

Let the agent investigate, approve, execute, and verify everything by itself.

Strong design

Use the agent for interpretation and investigation. Use deterministic workflows for financial, destructive, or compliance-sensitive execution.

Business example
Agent:
Determines package is lost
Finds replacement is allowed

Workflow:
Creates replacement
Updates inventory
Notifies customer

When to use

  • Customer support
  • Claims handling
  • Incident response
  • Supply-chain exceptions

Common mistakes

  • No clear boundary.
  • Workflow rules duplicated in prompts.
  • Agent bypasses controls.
  • Workflow called too early.
Mini exercise

Which component should usually process a final payment?

Answer: A deterministic workflow.

Memory line

Let agents decide where judgment is needed and workflows execute where control is required.

Simple meaning

Autonomy is not all-or-nothing. It can be introduced in levels.

LevelCapability
0Answer only
1Recommend next action
2Perform read-only actions
3Perform reversible write actions
4Perform approved high-impact actions
5Broad autonomous operation
Weak design

Give the agent full autonomy from day one.

Strong design

Start with recommendations and read-only access. Increase autonomy only after evaluations, guardrails, and approvals are proven.

Example
Level 1: Recommend refund
Level 2: Check payment and policy
Level 3: Create refund draft
Level 4: Execute approved refund

Common mistakes

  • No formal levels.
  • Write access too early.
  • No rollback path.
  • No measurement before expansion.
Mini exercise

Safer for a new agent: send email or create a draft?

Answer: Create a draft.

Memory line

Autonomy should be earned gradually, not granted immediately.

Simple meaning

Some decisions should always remain rule-based.

Refund limit
Permission check
Retention rule
Retry limit
Approval requirement
State transition
Weak prompt

Use your judgment to decide whether approval is required.

Strong prompt

If the refund amount exceeds ₹10,000, approval is mandatory.

Developer example
if amount > autonomous_limit:
    status = "waiting_for_approval"

Common mistakes

  • Exact thresholds only in prompts.
  • Rules not tested.
  • Model allowed to override checks.
Memory line

Keep exact policy decisions outside probabilistic reasoning.

Simple meaning

Some decisions benefit from model reasoning because they involve ambiguity or interpretation.

What does the user want?
Which issue is most important?
Which tool should investigate next?
Which explanation fits evidence?
What clarification is needed?
Which exception path applies?
Weak prompt

Let rules handle every customer message.

Strong prompt

Use model reasoning only for ambiguous interpretation, prioritization, and dynamic evidence gathering.

Example
User:
The order came, but something is wrong.

Agent clarification:
Is the item damaged, missing,
incorrect, or incomplete?

Common mistake

Trying to represent every natural-language situation with fixed rules.

Memory line

Use agent reasoning where meaning is uncertain, not where policy is exact.

Simple meaning

Workflows optimize predictability. Agents optimize flexibility.

More Flexibility → More Validation Needed
Weak design

Maximize autonomy and handle errors later.

Strong design

Use the minimum autonomy needed for the task and add stronger validation as flexibility increases.

Example
Workflow:
Always follows the approved refund sequence

Agent:
Investigates unusual exceptions
but cannot bypass refund controls

Common mistakes

  • Flexibility treated as the main goal.
  • Predictability ignored.
  • No risk-based control.
  • No human fallback.
Memory line

Flexibility is valuable only when it is controlled.

Simple meaning

Workflow testing verifies known paths, rules, retries, and state transitions.

What to test

  • Happy path
  • Invalid input
  • Permission failure
  • Approval required
  • Timeout
  • Duplicate request
  • Rollback
  • Final state
Weak test

Check whether the workflow works.

Strong test

Test every branch, failure path, state transition, retry rule, and approval condition.

Developer example
Test:
Refund above limit

Expected:
waiting_for_approval

Must not:
call payment execution tool

Common mistakes

  • Happy path only.
  • No idempotency tests.
  • No rollback tests.
  • No invalid-state tests.
Memory line

Workflows are predictable only when every important path is tested.

Simple meaning

Agent testing evaluates decisions across many possible situations.

What to evaluate

Goal completion
Tool selection
Unnecessary calls
Safety
Cost and latency
Escalation quality
Weak test

Did the final answer look good?

Strong test

Evaluate whether the agent chose appropriate tools, avoided unsafe actions, used evidence, stayed within budget, and completed or escalated correctly.

Example
Scenario:
Courier status unavailable

Acceptable:
Retry once
Use approved fallback
Escalate

Unacceptable:
Invent delivery status

Common mistakes

  • Only final text evaluated.
  • Tool trace ignored.
  • No adversarial cases.
  • No reproducible test set.
Mini exercise

Can two agents take different paths and both be correct?

Answer: Yes, if both satisfy safety and goal criteria.

Memory line

Test agents by outcome and behavior, not only by wording.

Simple meaning

Workflow failures are usually operational. Agent failures may also be behavioral.

Workflow failureTimeout, invalid state, missing approval, API error.
Agent failureWrong tool, poor plan, loops, ignored evidence, unsafe conclusion.
Weak design

Use the same failure handling for both.

Strong design

Use deterministic recovery for workflow failures and behavior monitoring, validation, and escalation for agent failures.

Example
Workflow failure:
Payment API timeout

Agent failure:
Repeatedly calls inventory tool
for a payment-status problem

Common mistakes

  • No distinction between system and reasoning failure.
  • No loop detection.
  • No safe fallback mode.
Memory line

Workflow failure is usually operational; agent failure may also be behavioral.

Simple meaning

Humans should review decisions where impact, uncertainty, or authority is high.

Weak design

Require approval for every action.

Strong design

Require human approval when financial impact exceeds limits, confidence is low, policy is ambiguous, action is irreversible, or legal/reputational risk is high.

Business example
Agent may:
Search policy
Retrieve order
Draft response

Human approval:
₹50,000 refund
Account suspension
Contract termination

Common mistakes

  • No threshold.
  • Approval too frequent.
  • Review too late.
  • Scope unclear.
Memory line

Human review should be risk-based, not universal or absent.

Simple meaning

Reversible actions can safely receive more autonomy than irreversible ones.

ActionReversibility
Search documentFully reversible
Create draftReversible
Update ticketUsually reversible
Send emailDifficult
Process paymentDifficult
Delete production dataPotentially irreversible
Weak design

Allow the agent to perform any write action.

Strong design

Grant autonomy first for read-only and reversible actions, and require stronger approval for irreversible ones.

Example
Safer:
create_email_draft

Higher risk:
send_email

Common mistakes

  • No reversibility classification.
  • Rollback not tested.
  • Draft and send treated equally.
Memory line

The less reversible the action, the less autonomous it should be.

Simple meaning

Workflows are usually faster and cheaper; agents are usually more flexible but more variable.

WorkflowFaster, cheaper, predictable, fewer model calls.
AgentMore flexible, more tools, higher tokens, variable latency.
Weak design

Use an agent even when a workflow can finish in one call.

Strong design

Use deterministic execution for known tasks and agent reasoning only where it adds measurable value.

Example
Password reset:
Workflow = 2 seconds
Agent investigation = unnecessary

Common mistakes

  • No token budget.
  • No tool-call limit.
  • Agent used for routine transactions.
  • Latency not measured.
Memory line

Autonomy should justify its additional cost and latency.

Simple meaning

A workflow can escalate to an agent when it encounters an unknown or conflicting case.

Normal request→Workflow→Known?→Continue or escalate
Weak design

Send every case directly to the agent.

Strong design

Handle standard cases with workflows and escalate only unknown, conflicting, or exceptional cases.

Support example
Normal:
Standard refund status lookup

Escalation:
Refund is successful in one system
and failed in another

Common mistakes

  • No escalation criteria.
  • Agent called too early.
  • Context lost during escalation.
  • Workflow cannot resume later.
Mini exercise

Two systems return conflicting payment status. Workflow or agent?

Answer: Escalate to an agent or human investigation path.

Memory line

Let workflows handle normal cases and agents handle exceptions.

Simple meaning

After investigation, the agent hands a structured decision to a controlled workflow.

Why it matters

The agent may interpret well but should not directly execute sensitive operations.

Weak handoff

Please refund this customer.

Strong handoff
{
  "workflow":"approved_refund",
  "order_id":"2056",
  "amount":2500,
  "reason":"package_lost",
  "approval_id":"AP88"
}
Developer example
Workflow validates:
order
amount
approval
duplicate status
permission

Common mistakes

  • Free-form handoff.
  • Missing approval reference.
  • Workflow trusts agent output blindly.
  • No schema validation.
Memory line

Agent reasoning should hand off structured intent, not unrestricted commands.

Stage 1 — Agent investigation
Check order
Check courier
Retrieve policy
Compare allowed options

Result:
Package lost
Replacement allowed
No approval required
Customer prefers replacement
Stage 2 — Structured handoff
{
  "workflow":"create_replacement",
  "order_id":"2056",
  "reason":"package_lost",
  "policy_version":"4.2",
  "approval_required":false
}
Stage 3 — Deterministic workflow
Validate order
Validate policy
Reserve inventory
Create replacement
Update order
Create notification
Verification
Replacement ID exists
Order updated
Notification sent
Goal complete
User Request
    ↓
Deterministic Router
    ↓
Known request?
 ├── Yes → Workflow
 └── No
       ↓
   Triage Agent
       ↓
   Investigation
       ↓
   Structured Decision
       ↓
   Policy Validator
       ↓
   Approval Gate
       ↓
   Deterministic Workflow
       ↓
   Result Verification
route = classify_request(request)

if route.is_known:
    result = run_workflow(route.workflow, request)
else:
    investigation = agent.investigate(
        goal=request,
        allowed_tools=read_only_tools
    )

    decision = validate_agent_decision(investigation)

    if decision.requires_approval:
        request_approval(decision)
    else:
        result = run_workflow(
            decision.workflow,
            decision.structured_input
        )

verify_result(result)
QuestionPrefer workflowPrefer agent
Are steps known?YesNo
Is route stable?YesNo
Is task ambiguous?NoYes
Dynamic tool choice?NoYes
High-risk action?ExecutionInvestigation only
Strict compliance?YesLimited autonomy
Exceptions common?NoYes
Cost sensitivity high?YesOnly if justified
Interpretive judgment needed?NoYes
Can hybrid work?OftenOften
Interpret with Agents → Validate with Rules → Execute with Workflows → Verify with Evidence
WorkflowStable, predictable, controlled execution.
AgentFlexible interpretation and investigation.
RulesExact policy and safety boundaries.
HybridBest balance of adaptability and control.
Final memory line

Workflows provide control, agents provide adaptability, and the best systems combine both deliberately.

Quick knowledge check

1. What is a deterministic workflow?
A predefined sequence controlled by rules.
2. What is an autonomous agent?
A system that dynamically selects actions from goals, state, and observations.
3. When should a workflow be preferred?
When steps are known, stable, and high control is required.
4. When should an agent be preferred?
When the task is ambiguous and the path must be discovered.
5. What is a hybrid system?
An agent handles interpretation while workflows handle controlled execution.
6. Why use autonomy levels?
To increase capability gradually according to risk and evidence.
7. Which decisions should remain deterministic?
Exact thresholds, permissions, approval rules, and state transitions.
8. Why are reversible actions safer?
Mistakes can be undone more easily.
9. What is agent-to-workflow handoff?
A structured validated decision sent to a controlled workflow.
10. Safest principle?
Use the minimum autonomy necessary and keep high-impact execution deterministic.

A single-agent system uses one central agent to understand the goal, choose actions, use tools, update state, handle failures, and verify completion.

A single agent is not a lone model. It is one decision-maker surrounded by tools, state, validation, permissions, and control.
GoalAgentValidated ActionState UpdateVerified Outcome

Simple meaning

A single-agent architecture uses one main agent to understand the goal, plan, choose tools, update state, handle errors, and decide when the task is complete.

Why it matters

One clear decision-maker reduces handoff errors, duplicate calls, conflicting decisions, and debugging complexity.

Weak prompt

Solve the problem using any tools available.

Strong prompt

Act as the primary task agent. Read the goal and state, identify the most important missing fact, select one approved action, validate the result, update state, and continue only if the goal is incomplete.

When to use

  • One domain is involved.
  • The task is moderately complex.
  • The toolset is manageable.
  • One agent can understand the full context.

When not to use

  • Many specialist domains are involved.
  • Different permissions are mandatory.
  • Independent roles must work in parallel.
  • Context has become too large.
Business example
Customer-support agent:
- Read complaint
- Check order
- Check courier
- Retrieve policy
- Recommend resolution
- Draft response

Common mistakes

  • Too many unrelated tools.
  • No responsibility boundary.
  • No structured state.
  • No stop condition.
Mini exercise

Which is better for one agent: resolving one delayed order or running finance, HR, legal, and security together?

Answer: Resolving one delayed order.

Memory line

A single-agent architecture works best when one agent can understand and control the full task.

Simple meaning

A single-agent system contains one reasoning agent surrounded by control components.

User interfaceReceives goals and displays results.
Agent controllerRuns the loop.
ModelInterprets and proposes actions.
Tool registryLists approved capabilities.
State storeTracks task progress.
ValidatorChecks actions and results.
Approval layerBlocks sensitive actions.
MemoryStores reusable context.
ObservabilityRecords trace and cost.
Developer example
The agent does not directly access the database.

It requests:
get_order_status(order_id)

The controller validates and executes the request.

Common mistake

Treating the LLM itself as the whole agent system.

Memory line

The model reasons, but the surrounding architecture provides control.

Simple meaning

The controller manages the agent loop and owns execution decisions.

Why it matters

It determines when to call the model, which tools are available, whether requests are valid, when state changes, and when to stop.

Weak design

Model directly calls any external system.

Strong design

The model proposes an action. The controller validates it. An approved tool executes. The controller validates the result and updates state.

Developer example
while not goal_complete(state):
    proposal = model.decide(state, tools)
    action = validate_proposal(proposal)
    result = execute(action)
    validate_result(result)
    update_state(state, result)

Common mistakes

  • No central loop control.
  • Tool output passed directly without validation.
  • Model controls retry count.
  • No maximum iteration limit.
Memory line

The controller owns execution; the model proposes decisions.

Simple meaning

The goal layer tells the agent what to achieve and what limits to respect.

Why it matters

Without a clear goal, the agent may optimize the wrong outcome.

Weak prompt

Help the customer.

Strong prompt
Resolve delayed Order 2056.

Success criteria:
- Cause verified
- Approved resolution completed
- Customer notified
- No critical task open

Constraints:
- Read-only investigation first
- No refund above ₹10,000 without approval
- Do not invent missing information

What the goal layer should include

  • Desired outcome
  • Success criteria
  • Scope
  • Constraints
  • Allowed and forbidden actions
  • Cost and time limits

Common mistakes

  • Broad goal only.
  • No success criteria.
  • No boundaries.
  • Conflicting instructions.
Memory line

A clear goal tells the agent what to achieve; constraints tell it how far it may go.

Simple meaning

The tool registry contains the approved tools available to the agent.

Why it matters

A single agent should not receive every tool in the organisation.

Example registry
[
  {
    "name":"get_order_status",
    "type":"read",
    "risk":"low"
  },
  {
    "name":"create_replacement",
    "type":"write",
    "risk":"medium"
  },
  {
    "name":"create_refund",
    "type":"write",
    "risk":"high",
    "approval_required":true
  }
]
Weak design

Provide shell, SQL, payment, email, file, and production tools to one unrestricted agent.

Strong design

Expose only narrow tools required for the current domain and role.

Common mistakes

  • Too many similar tools.
  • Vague descriptions.
  • No risk classification.
  • Read and write tools mixed without controls.
Memory line

A smaller, clearer toolset usually produces safer and more accurate decisions.

Simple meaning

State records what the agent currently knows and what has already happened.

Why it matters

The reasoning loop depends on reliable, current task state.

Example state
{
  "goal":"Resolve delayed Order 2056",
  "status":"in_progress",
  "known_facts":{
    "order_status":"dispatched",
    "tracking_id":"TRK9087",
    "courier_status":"package_lost"
  },
  "completed_tasks":[
    "retrieve_order",
    "check_courier"
  ],
  "pending_tasks":[
    "retrieve_policy",
    "select_resolution"
  ],
  "attempt_count":2
}
Weak prompt

Remember the task from the conversation.

Strong prompt

Use structured state as the source of truth. Before every action, check known facts, completed tasks, pending tasks, constraints, and attempt count.

Common mistakes

  • Conversation history used as state.
  • Old values not replaced.
  • Completed tasks not recorded.
  • No blocked status.
Memory line

State is the agent’s operational truth for the current task.

Simple meaning

Memory helps the agent reuse relevant information beyond the current step.

Working memory
Session memory
Long-term memory
Procedural memory
Weak design

Store everything the user says forever.

Strong design

Store only verified, useful, authorized, and properly scoped information.

Example
Working memory:
Current order status

Procedural memory:
Permission errors are non-retryable

Long-term preference:
User prefers drafts before sending

Common mistakes

  • Temporary state stored as long-term memory.
  • Sensitive information stored unnecessarily.
  • Old policy remembered as current truth.
  • No expiry.
Memory line

Use state for the current task and memory only for reusable context.

Simple meaning

The agent may create and maintain its own short plan.

Why it matters

The agent must keep the goal, dependencies, and progress aligned.

Weak prompt

Create a complete detailed plan before doing anything.

Strong prompt

Create a short high-level plan, execute one ready task at a time, and replan only when new evidence changes the path.

Example
Plan:
1. Retrieve order
2. Check shipment
3. Retrieve policy
4. Select resolution
5. Execute
6. Notify

Common mistakes

  • Planning too far ahead.
  • No plan validation.
  • No replanning.
  • Plan and state become inconsistent.
Memory line

A single agent should plan enough to guide execution, not enough to predict every future detail.

Simple meaning

The central loop combines state reading, reasoning, action, observation, and update.

Read state→Identify gap→Choose action→Validate→Execute→Update
Weak design

Generate a long answer and several actions at once.

Strong design

Choose one justified next action, observe the result, update state, and decide again.

Developer example
proposal = agent.next_action(goal, state, tools)
action = validator.check(proposal)
observation = executor.run(action)
state = updater.apply(state, observation)

Common mistakes

  • Action selected without reading state.
  • Observation not validated.
  • Multiple dependent writes at once.
  • No progress check.
Memory line

The quality of a single agent depends on the discipline of its loop.

Simple meaning

The validation layer checks proposed actions before execution and tool results afterward.

Before executionTool exists, schema valid, permission available, business rules pass, approval present, no duplicate.
After executionCorrect entity, valid schema, fresh result, expected status, no partial failure, safe state update.
Weak design

Execute every tool request generated by the model.

Strong design

Treat every proposed action as untrusted until deterministic validation passes.

Example
Proposed refund: ₹25,000
Agent limit: ₹10,000
Validation:
Block execution and request approval
Memory line

A single agent becomes trustworthy only when model decisions are surrounded by deterministic checks.

Simple meaning

The approval layer blocks sensitive actions until an authorized person or system approves them.

Why it matters

One agent should not approve its own high-impact recommendation.

Weak design

The agent decides whether its own action is safe.

Strong design

The agent recommends. The policy engine decides whether approval is required. An authorized human or system approves.

Example
Action:
Refund ₹25,000

Result:
waiting_for_approval

Common mistakes

  • Approval checked after execution.
  • Silence treated as approval.
  • Approval reused for a different action.
  • Agent creates its own approval token.
Memory line

The agent may recommend a sensitive action, but it should not authorize itself.

Simple meaning

The agent must respond safely to tool, reasoning, and state failures.

Timeout
Invalid argument
Permission denied
Conflicting data
State corruption
Cost limit reached
Weak prompt

Keep trying until the task succeeds.

Strong prompt

Retry only retryable failures within budget, use approved fallback where available, escalate permission and state errors, and stop when safe progress is impossible.

Example
Courier API timeout
→ retry once
→ fallback endpoint
→ escalate if still unavailable

Common mistakes

  • Infinite retries.
  • Same invalid argument reused.
  • No fallback.
  • Failure state not recorded.
Memory line

A reliable agent must know how to fail safely.

Simple meaning

Loop prevention stops repeated actions that do not change state or improve goal progress.

Same tool repeated
Same result returned
No state change
Attempt count rising
No goal progress
Budget ignored
Weak design

Continue while the goal is incomplete.

Strong design

Continue only if a new action is available, state can change, retry budget remains, and progress toward the goal is possible.

Practical controls

  • Maximum iterations
  • Per-tool retry limits
  • Duplicate-action detection
  • State-difference checks
  • Cost and time limits
Mini exercise

The same order-status result appears three times. What next?

Answer: Stop repeating and choose another source or escalate.

Memory line

An incomplete goal does not justify endless repetition.

Simple meaning

The agent needs explicit rules for when to stop.

Stop when

  • Success criteria are satisfied.
  • Human approval is required.
  • Required information is unavailable.
  • No safe action remains.
  • Retry, cost, or time budget is exhausted.
Weak prompt

Stop when you think the task is done.

Strong prompt

Stop only when all success criteria are verified, or safe progress is blocked, or a defined approval or budget boundary is reached.

Example
Root cause verified: Yes
Resolution completed: Yes
Customer notified: Yes
Open critical tasks: No

Stop: Goal complete

Common mistakes

  • Task completion confused with goal completion.
  • No blocked state.
  • Agent continues because tools are available.
  • Same fact repeatedly verified.
Memory line

A good agent knows when continuing is useful and when stopping is safer.

Simple meaning

Observability records what the agent did, why it did it, and what changed.

What to record

  • Goal and state snapshot
  • Selected action
  • Short justification
  • Sanitized arguments
  • Validation result
  • Observation and state change
  • Cost, duration, retry count
  • Stop or continue decision
Weak logging

Agent completed the task.

Strong logging
Cycle 3
Goal: Resolve delayed order
Action: search_replacement_policy
Reason: Policy missing
Result: replacement_allowed=true
State change: replacement_eligible=true
Duration: 380 ms

Common mistakes

  • Logging secrets.
  • No correlation ID.
  • No state changes recorded.
  • Only final answer logged.
Memory line

A single-agent system should leave a trace clear enough to explain every important action.

Simple meaning

Single-agent systems are often simpler and more practical than multi-agent systems.

One decision-maker
Lower orchestration cost
Fewer handoff errors
Simpler state
Easier debugging
Lower token cost
Clear accountability
Easier evaluation
Example

One support agent using five approved tools is usually simpler than five agents passing messages to each other.

Memory line

Start with one agent unless multiple agents provide a measurable benefit.

Simple meaning

A single agent can become too broad as context, tools, and responsibilities grow.

Large context
Too many tools
Permission complexity
Reduced specialization
Difficult parallelism
Role conflicts
Greater blast radius
Harder isolation
Weak design

One agent manages all enterprise systems.

Strong design

Keep one agent within a clear domain, tool boundary, and permission scope.

Example
Good:
Customer-support agent

Too broad:
Support + Finance + Legal + Security + HR agent

Common mistake

Adding more prompt instructions to compensate for an architecture that has become too broad.

Memory line

A single agent should have one coherent responsibility, not every responsibility.

Simple meaning

Split into multiple agents only when role separation improves safety, scale, or quality.

Good reasons to split

  • Distinct expertise
  • Different permissions
  • Independent parallel work
  • Separate accountability
  • Context isolation
  • Different models or tools
Weak reason

Multi-agent sounds more advanced.

Strong reason

Finance and legal tasks require different permissions, policies, and validation paths.

Example
Research agent
Data-analysis agent
Report reviewer

Use only when each role is truly distinct.

Common mistakes

  • Splitting too early.
  • Overlapping roles.
  • No handoff schema.
  • Higher cost with no quality gain.
Memory line

Split one agent into several only when separation improves safety, scale, or quality.

Initial state
Goal known
Order status unknown
Courier status unknown
Resolution unknown
Cycle 1
Action:
get_order_status("2056")

Observation:
dispatched
tracking_id=TRK9087
Cycle 2
Action:
get_courier_status("TRK9087")

Observation:
package_lost
Cycle 3
Action:
search_replacement_policy("package_lost")

Observation:
replacement allowed
approval not required
Cycle 4
Action:
create_replacement(
  order_id="2056",
  idempotency_key="order2056-replacement-v1"
)

Observation:
replacement_id=RP88
status=success
Cycle 5
Action:
create_customer_email_draft("2056")

Observation:
draft created
Completion check
Cause verified: Yes
Resolution completed: Yes
Customer notified: Yes
Open critical actions: No

Goal complete
User Request
    ↓
Goal and Constraint Builder
    ↓
Single-Agent Controller
    ↓
Model
    ↓
Proposed Action
    ↓
Tool and Policy Validator
    ↓
Approval Gate
    ↓
Tool Executor
    ↓
Observation Validator
    ↓
State Store
    ↓
Stop / Continue / Escalate
state = initialize_state(user_request)

while True:
    proposal = agent.next_action(
        goal=state.goal,
        state=state,
        tools=allowed_tools
    )

    action = validate_action(
        proposal,
        permissions=permissions,
        policies=policies
    )

    if action.requires_approval:
        state.status = "waiting_for_approval"
        break

    result = execute_tool(action)
    validate_result(result)
    update_state(state, result)

    if goal_complete(state):
        state.status = "complete"
        break

    if should_escalate(state):
        state.status = "escalated"
        break
AreaQuestion
GoalIs the outcome clear?
ScopeIs one agent responsible for a coherent domain?
ToolsAre only necessary tools exposed?
StateIs task state structured and current?
MemoryIs reusable memory properly scoped?
PlanningIs the plan short and adaptable?
ValidationAre actions and results checked?
ApprovalAre sensitive actions blocked correctly?
ErrorsAre retry and fallback rules defined?
LoopsAre iteration and duplicate limits present?
StopAre success and blocked conditions explicit?
ObservabilityCan every decision be traced?
CostAre token and tool budgets defined?
EscalationIs there a safe fallback?
Goal → Single Agent → Validated Action → Observation → State Update → Verified Completion
ResponsibilityOne coherent domain.
ControlController, validation, and approvals.
StateStructured operational truth.
SafetyLimits, tracing, and escalation.
Final memory line

A single agent is one clear decision-maker inside strong deterministic boundaries.

Quick knowledge check

1. What is a single-agent architecture?
One central agent manages the task from goal to completion.
2. Is the LLM the whole agent system?
No. The system also needs control, tools, state, validation, and observability.
3. What does the agent controller do?
It manages the reasoning and action loop.
4. Why use a limited tool registry?
To improve safety, clarity, and tool selection.
5. What is structured state?
A reliable record of current facts, tasks, attempts, and progress.
6. Why is validation required?
Model proposals and tool results may be invalid or unsafe.
7. Should the agent approve its own sensitive action?
No.
8. Why are loop limits important?
They prevent repeated actions without progress.
9. When should one agent be split?
When specialization, permissions, scale, or accountability clearly require it.
10. Safest principle?
Keep one clear decision-maker inside strong deterministic boundaries.

A multi-agent system coordinates specialised agents with clear roles, shared state, structured handoffs, scoped permissions, and one verified outcome.

The main benefit is not “more intelligence.” It is separation of responsibility.
GoalCoordinatorSpecialistsHandoffsVerified Outcome

Simple meaning

A multi-agent architecture uses more than one agent, with each agent handling a specific role or part of the task.

Why it matters

One agent may become overloaded when too many tools, permissions, skills, contexts, or independent workstreams are involved.

Weak prompt

Create many agents and let them collaborate.

Strong prompt

Create separate agents only for responsibilities that require distinct expertise, tools, permissions, or independent validation. Define each role, input, output, and handoff clearly.

When to use

  • Roles are genuinely distinct.
  • Different permissions are required.
  • Work can run independently.
  • Separate review adds value.
  • Context isolation is useful.

When not to use

  • One agent can handle the task clearly.
  • Roles overlap heavily.
  • Handoffs cost more than they help.
  • The task is small.
Business example
Monthly sales investigation:
- Data Retrieval Agent
- Analysis Agent
- Recommendation Agent
- Review Agent

Common mistakes

  • Creating agents only because it sounds advanced.
  • Overlapping responsibilities.
  • No ownership.
  • No handoff schema.
  • No final authority.
Mini exercise

Which is a stronger reason for two agents: “more intelligence” or separate finance and legal permissions?

Answer: Separate finance and legal permissions.

Memory line

Use multiple agents only when separation improves safety, scale, or quality.

Simple meaning

A single agent manages one coherent task. A multi-agent system divides work among specialised agents.

AreaSingle agentMulti-agent
Decision-makerOneSeveral
ArchitectureSimplerMore complex
ContextCentralCan be isolated
SpecialisationLimitedHigher
HandoffsFewMany
CostLowerHigher
DebuggingEasierHarder
ParallelismLimitedStronger
Weak design

Split one task into many agents automatically.

Strong design

Start with one agent. Split only when role separation creates measurable value.

Example
Single agent:
Resolve one delayed order

Multi-agent:
Investigate enterprise-wide disruption
across logistics, inventory, suppliers, and finance

Common mistake

Treating multi-agent as a universal upgrade.

Memory line

Multi-agent systems trade simplicity for separation and scale.

Simple meaning

A multi-agent system needs shared coordination, state, role definitions, permissions, and validation.

CoordinatorAssigns and tracks work.
Agent registryLists capabilities.
Role definitionsSet boundaries.
Shared stateStores progress.
Handoff protocolTransfers work.
Task queueHolds pending tasks.
Permission modelScopes tools and data.
Conflict resolverHandles disagreement.
ObservabilityRecords trace and cost.

Common mistake

Using several agents without a shared task model and one source of truth.

Memory line

Multiple agents need shared coordination, not just separate prompts.

Simple meaning

Each agent should have one clear responsibility.

Good role definition

Role
Goal
Allowed tools
Input schema
Output schema
Permissions
Stop condition
Escalation rule
Weak role

Research Agent: Find useful information.

Strong role
Research Agent

Goal:
Find authoritative evidence for the sales decline.

Allowed tools:
Document search
Approved web search
Internal reports

Output:
Source list
Key findings
Confidence
Open questions

Forbidden:
Financial execution
Final business decision
Developer example
{
  "agent":"inventory_analyst",
  "responsibility":"analyse stockout impact",
  "allowed_tools":[
    "get_inventory_history",
    "get_lost_sales"
  ],
  "output_schema":{
    "finding":"string",
    "evidence":"array",
    "confidence":"number"
  }
}

Common mistakes

  • Roles defined only by names.
  • Several agents do the same work.
  • No forbidden actions.
  • No completion criteria.
Mini exercise

Is “Operations Agent” a clear role?

Answer: No. Responsibility, tools, outputs, and limits are missing.

Memory line

A role is useful only when responsibility and boundaries are explicit.

Simple meaning

The coordinator manages the main goal and assigns work to specialist agents.

Responsibilities

  • Understand the goal
  • Break work into tasks
  • Assign owners
  • Track status
  • Merge outputs
  • Resolve blockers
  • Decide when to stop
Weak prompt

Tell other agents what to do.

Strong prompt

Create a task list from the main goal. Assign each task to the agent whose capability, permissions, and context best match it. Track dependencies and accept only structured outputs.

Example
Goal: Explain sales decline

Coordinator assigns:
Inventory Agent → stockouts
Pricing Agent → price changes
Campaign Agent → traffic
Reviewer → compare evidence

Common mistakes

  • Coordinator performs every specialist task.
  • No task status.
  • No dependency management.
  • All outputs trusted automatically.
Memory line

The coordinator manages the work; specialists perform the work.

Simple meaning

A supervisor decides which worker agent should act next and keeps central control.

User→Supervisor→Worker→Supervisor→Continue or stop
Weak prompt

Ask any agent to help.

Strong prompt

Select exactly one worker based on required expertise, tool, permission level, and current task state.

Example
Current task:
Verify inventory shortage

Selected worker:
Inventory Agent

When to use

  • One central authority is desirable.
  • Agents have clear specialties.
  • Execution is mostly sequential.

When not to use

It can become a bottleneck when many tasks are independent.

Common mistakes

  • Supervisor overloaded.
  • Worker result not validated.
  • Same worker repeatedly selected.
  • No fallback when no worker fits.
Memory line

The supervisor chooses the next specialist while keeping central control.

Simple meaning

A router sends a request directly to the appropriate agent.

Weak prompt

Choose whichever agent sounds suitable.

Strong prompt

Classify the request into one approved agent role and return selected agent, confidence, reason, and whether clarification is needed.

Example
Refund question → Finance Agent
Contract question → Legal Agent
Delivery question → Logistics Agent
Developer example
{
  "selected_agent":"logistics_agent",
  "confidence":0.94,
  "reason":"Request concerns delayed shipment"
}

When to use

  • Requests map to clear domains.
  • Only one specialist is usually required.
  • Low orchestration overhead is preferred.

Common mistakes

  • Router invents agent names.
  • No confidence threshold.
  • Multi-intent request sent to one agent.
  • No fallback.
Memory line

A router chooses the destination; it does not manage a complex plan.

Simple meaning

A planner creates the task structure and workers execute bounded assignments.

Goal→Planner→Task list→Workers→Results
Weak prompt

Planner, solve the full task yourself.

Strong prompt

Planner: create tasks, dependencies, owners, and success criteria. Workers: execute only assigned tasks and return structured results.

Example
Planner:
T1 Retrieve sales data
T2 Analyse inventory
T3 Analyse pricing
T4 Compare findings

Workers execute T1–T3.
Reviewer handles T4.

Common mistakes

  • Planner also executes tasks.
  • Workers change the goal.
  • No dependency model.
  • Results not linked to task IDs.
Memory line

The planner defines the work; workers execute bounded tasks.

Simple meaning

Agents communicate directly without one supervisor controlling every exchange.

Weak design

Let agents talk until they agree.

Strong design

Allow direct communication only through structured messages, with a maximum number of exchanges and clear ownership.

Example
Research Agent
      ↕
Analysis Agent
      ↕
Reviewer Agent

When to use

  • Roles need mutual refinement.
  • Negotiation is part of the task.
  • Tasks are highly interdependent.

When not to use

  • Strong authority is required.
  • High-risk actions are involved.
  • Simple auditability is required.

Common mistakes

  • Endless discussion.
  • No final decision-maker.
  • Conflicting state versions.
  • Repeated information exchange.
Memory line

Peer collaboration needs strict message and stopping rules.

Simple meaning

Shared state gives all agents a common view of tasks, facts, owners, and progress.

Example
{
  "goal":"Investigate sales decline",
  "tasks":{
    "T1":{
      "owner":"inventory_agent",
      "status":"complete"
    },
    "T2":{
      "owner":"pricing_agent",
      "status":"in_progress"
    }
  },
  "facts":{
    "north_stockout_rate":0.31
  },
  "open_questions":[
    "Did price change affect conversion?"
  ]
}
Weak design

Each agent remembers the project independently.

Strong design

Use a shared structured state store. Agents may read relevant state and update only authorized fields.

Common mistakes

  • Multiple sources of truth.
  • Agents overwrite each other.
  • No versioning.
  • No field ownership.
  • Stale state.
Memory line

Multi-agent coordination depends on one trusted shared state.

Simple meaning

Each agent may need private working state that other agents do not see.

Why it matters

Private state supports context isolation, security, reduced noise, and specialist reasoning.

Example
Shared state:
Order ID, status, task progress

Finance private state:
Payment details

Legal private state:
Contract interpretation notes
Weak design

Share every piece of context with every agent.

Strong design

Share only information required for coordination. Keep sensitive or role-specific context private.

Common mistakes

  • Sensitive data exposed unnecessarily.
  • Private assumptions treated as shared facts.
  • No boundary between shared and private state.
Memory line

Share coordination facts broadly; keep specialist context scoped.

Simple meaning

A handoff transfers responsibility from one agent to another.

Strong handoff structure

Task ID
Goal
Current state
Completed work
Required input
Expected output
Constraints
Escalation rule
Weak handoff

Please continue from here.

Strong handoff
{
  "task_id":"T4",
  "from_agent":"research_agent",
  "to_agent":"analysis_agent",
  "goal":"Rank causes of sales decline",
  "inputs":{
    "inventory_finding":"High stockout correlation",
    "pricing_finding":"Minor price impact"
  },
  "expected_output":{
    "ranked_causes":"array",
    "confidence":"number"
  }
}

Common mistakes

  • Free-form handoffs.
  • Missing task ID.
  • No expected output.
  • No ownership transfer.
  • No acknowledgment.
Memory line

A handoff should transfer structured responsibility, not just text.

Simple meaning

A communication protocol defines how agents exchange work and status.

TASK_ASSIGNMENT
TASK_RESULT
CLARIFICATION_REQUEST
BLOCKED
APPROVAL_REQUIRED
FINAL_REVIEW
Example
{
  "message_type":"TASK_RESULT",
  "task_id":"T2",
  "agent":"pricing_agent",
  "status":"complete",
  "result":{
    "finding":"Price increase caused minor impact",
    "confidence":0.68
  }
}
Weak design

Agents send natural-language messages only.

Strong design

Use structured messages with required fields and validate every message before processing.

Common mistakes

  • Message type missing.
  • No schema.
  • Duplicate result.
  • Untrusted content treated as command.
  • No sender identity.
Memory line

Reliable agent communication is structured, typed, and validated.

Simple meaning

Conflict resolution handles inconsistent facts, recommendations, or confidence levels.

Resolution methods

  • Prefer authoritative sources.
  • Compare evidence freshness and completeness.
  • Request more verification.
  • Apply deterministic policy.
  • Escalate to reviewer or human.
Weak prompt

Choose whichever agent sounds more confident.

Strong prompt

Resolve disagreement using source authority, freshness, completeness, confidence basis, and applicable policy. Escalate if the conflict remains material.

Example
Inventory Agent:
Stockout caused decline.

Pricing Agent:
Price increase caused decline.

Reviewer:
Inventory evidence is stronger in North region.
Pricing impact is limited nationally.

Common mistakes

  • Majority vote without evidence.
  • Most verbose agent wins.
  • Disagreement hidden.
  • No final authority.
Memory line

Resolve conflicts with evidence and policy, not confidence of tone.

Simple meaning

Independent agents can work at the same time to reduce latency.

Example
Inventory Agent ─┐
Pricing Agent   ──┼→ Reviewer
Campaign Agent  ──┘

When to use

  • Tasks are independent.
  • Required inputs are ready.
  • Outputs can be merged later.
  • Tool limits allow it.

When not to use

  • One task depends on another.
  • Agents may update the same data.
  • Write actions conflict.
  • Cost is too high.
Weak design

Run every agent at once.

Strong design

Run only independent read-only tasks in parallel. Synchronize before shared decisions or write actions.

Common mistakes

  • Dependent work parallelized.
  • Conflicting writes.
  • No merge step.
  • Duplicate data retrieval.
Memory line

Parallelize independent analysis, not dependent or conflicting execution.

Simple meaning

Sequential execution runs agents in a defined order when later work depends on earlier output.

Example
Research Agent
    ↓
Analysis Agent
    ↓
Reviewer Agent
    ↓
Execution Workflow
Weak design

Let agents choose their own order.

Strong design

Define dependencies explicitly and start an agent only when required inputs are complete.

Common mistakes

  • Agent starts with incomplete input.
  • No dependency status.
  • Reviewer starts too early.
  • Downstream agent uses stale output.
Memory line

Sequential execution preserves dependencies and evidence flow.

Simple meaning

Each agent should receive only the tools and data required for its role.

Example
Research Agent:
Read documents

Finance Agent:
Read payment data
Create refund draft

Approval Agent:
Approve according to policy

Execution Workflow:
Process approved refund
Weak design

All agents can use all tools.

Strong design

Apply least privilege per role. Do not let one agent investigate, approve, and execute the same high-impact action.

Common mistakes

  • Shared global credentials.
  • No role-based access.
  • Agent escalates its own permissions.
  • Approval agent can execute payments.
Memory line

Multi-agent design should separate authority as well as responsibility.

Simple meaning

A multi-agent system must handle both task failures and coordination failures.

Worker tool failure
Invalid result
Agent unavailable
Handoff rejected
Shared-state corruption
Timeout waiting
Weak design

Retry the entire multi-agent workflow.

Strong design

Retry only the failed bounded task, preserve successful outputs, and use a fallback agent or escalate when needed.

Example
Pricing Agent fails.
Inventory and Campaign results remain valid.
Retry or replace only Pricing Agent.

Common mistakes

  • Restarting everything.
  • Losing completed work.
  • No timeout.
  • No fallback agent.
  • Failure ownership unclear.
Memory line

Recover the smallest failed unit instead of repeating the whole system.

Simple meaning

Multi-agent loops happen when agents repeatedly delegate or respond without state change.

Warning pattern
Agent A delegates to B
Agent B delegates back to A
No state change
Same message repeated
No task completes

Controls

  • Maximum handoff count
  • Maximum message count
  • Single task owner
  • Duplicate-message detection
  • State-change requirement
  • Time and cost budgets
Weak prompt

Continue collaborating until everyone agrees.

Strong prompt

Limit collaboration to two exchange cycles. Stop if no new evidence or state change appears and escalate unresolved conflict.

Common mistakes

  • Endless debate.
  • Delegation ping-pong.
  • No final decision-maker.
  • Same task owned by several agents.
Memory line

Agent communication must produce state change or stop.

Simple meaning

Observability tracks the complete multi-agent execution.

What to record

  • Main goal
  • Task assignments
  • Agent identity
  • Inputs and outputs
  • Handoffs
  • Tool calls
  • State changes
  • Conflicts
  • Cost and latency
  • Retry count
Weak logging

Several agents completed the task.

Strong logging
Task T2
Owner: Inventory Agent
Status: Complete
Finding: Stockout correlation 0.82
Duration: 1.4 s
Cost: 1 tool call
Handoff: Reviewer Agent

Common mistakes

  • No task IDs.
  • Agent identity not logged.
  • No shared correlation ID.
  • Cost not separated by agent.
  • Only final answer stored.
Memory line

Multi-agent systems require task-level and agent-level tracing.

Simple meaning

Evaluation checks whether several agents outperform a simpler baseline.

What to measure

Goal completion
Correctness
Handoff accuracy
Duplicate work
Conflict rate
Cost and latency
Failure recovery
Safety
Human escalation
Weak evaluation

The agents produced a detailed answer.

Strong evaluation

Compare multi-agent with a single-agent baseline for quality improvement, cost increase, latency increase, error reduction, and safety improvement.

Common mistakes

  • No baseline.
  • More text treated as better quality.
  • Handoff cost ignored.
  • No reliability testing.
Mini exercise

Quality improves 1%, but cost and latency triple. Is multi-agent automatically better?

Answer: No.

Memory line

Multi-agent complexity must produce measurable value.

Coordinator
T1 Inventory analysis
T2 Pricing analysis
T3 Campaign analysis
T4 Findings review
T5 Final recommendations
Inventory Agent
Finding:
North-region stockouts strongly correlate with lost sales.

Confidence:
High
Pricing Agent
Finding:
Price increase caused a small conversion decline.

Confidence:
Medium
Campaign Agent
Finding:
Campaign traffic declined by 35%.

Confidence:
High
Reviewer Agent
Ranked causes:
1. Stockouts
2. Campaign decline
3. Pricing impact
Recommendation Agent
1. Rebalance inventory to North region
2. Restore high-performing campaigns
3. Test targeted price rollback
Completion check
Evidence attached: Yes
Causes ranked: Yes
Recommendations produced: Yes
Conflicts resolved: Yes
Goal complete
User Goal
    ↓
Coordinator
    ↓
Task Decomposer
    ↓
Agent Registry
    ↓
Task Assignment
    ↓
Specialist Agents
    ↓
Structured Results
    ↓
Shared State
    ↓
Reviewer / Conflict Resolver
    ↓
Policy and Approval Layer
    ↓
Controlled Workflow
    ↓
Final Verification
tasks = coordinator.decompose(goal)

for task in tasks:
    agent = registry.select(
        capability=task.capability,
        permissions=task.permissions
    )
    queue.assign(task, agent)

results = execute_ready_tasks(
    tasks=tasks,
    parallel=True
)

validated_results = [
    validate_agent_result(result)
    for result in results
]

decision = reviewer.combine(validated_results)
validate_decision(decision)

if decision.requires_execution:
    run_controlled_workflow(decision)

verify_goal_completion()
AreaQuestion
NeedIs multi-agent architecture truly necessary?
RolesDoes each agent have one clear responsibility?
CoordinatorWho owns the main goal?
ToolsAre tools scoped per agent?
StateIs there one trusted shared state?
PrivacyWhat remains private to each agent?
TasksAre task IDs and dependencies defined?
HandoffsAre messages structured and validated?
ConflictHow are disagreements resolved?
ParallelismWhich tasks are truly independent?
PermissionsIs authority separated by role?
FailureCan one failed task be retried independently?
LoopsAre handoff and message limits present?
ObservabilityCan every agent action be traced?
EvaluationDoes it outperform a single-agent baseline?
StopWho decides the goal is complete?
Goal → Coordinator → Specialized Agents → Structured Handoffs → Validated Combined Result
RolesDistinct responsibility and authority.
CoordinationOne owner for the overall goal.
StateShared truth plus private context.
ControlValidation, limits, tracing, and evaluation.
Final memory line

A multi-agent system is a coordinated organisation of specialised decision-makers with clear roles, shared state, structured handoffs, and one verified outcome.

Quick knowledge check

1. What is a multi-agent architecture?
A system where several specialised agents collaborate on one larger goal.
2. Why use several agents?
For role separation, specialist expertise, permission isolation, and parallel work.
3. What does the coordinator do?
It decomposes, assigns, tracks, and combines work.
4. What is a supervisor pattern?
A central agent selects which worker acts next.
5. What is a router pattern?
A component sends a request directly to the appropriate specialist.
6. Why is shared state important?
It gives all agents a consistent source of truth.
7. What should a handoff contain?
Task ID, goal, state, inputs, expected output, constraints, and ownership.
8. How should conflicts be resolved?
Using evidence quality, source authority, policy, and escalation.
9. Why compare with a single-agent baseline?
To prove added complexity improves quality, safety, or scale.
10. Safest principle?
Use specialised agents only when clear separation creates measurable value.

Reliable collaboration depends on explicit ownership, structured inputs and outputs, validated handoffs, scoped permissions, and clear stopping rules.

Good multi-agent collaboration is not agents talking more. It is agents owning clear work, transferring responsibility safely, and producing one verified outcome.
Define RoleAssign TaskProduce OutputTransfer OwnershipVerify Completion

Simple meaning

An agent role defines the responsibility assigned to one agent.

Why it matters

Without clear roles, agents duplicate work, use the wrong tools, override each other, and produce incompatible outputs.

Weak role

Operations Agent: Handle operational problems.

Strong role
Inventory Analysis Agent

Goal:
Determine whether stock availability contributed
to the sales decline.

Allowed tools:
- get_inventory_history
- get_stockout_events
- get_lost_sales

Output:
- finding
- evidence
- confidence
- unresolved questions

Forbidden:
- Change inventory
- Create purchase orders
- Make final business decisions

When to use separate roles

  • Different expertise
  • Different tools
  • Different permissions
  • Independent validation
  • Separate accountability

Common mistakes

  • Role is only a title.
  • No input or output definition.
  • No forbidden actions.
  • Several agents own the same task.
Mini exercise

Is “Business Agent” a sufficient role definition?

Answer: No.

Memory line

A role is complete only when ownership, authority, input, and output are explicit.

Simple meaning

Responsibility defines the result an agent owns.

Weak responsibility

Review inventory data.

Strong responsibility

Determine whether inventory shortages materially contributed to lost sales in the North region.

Business example
Weak:
Analyse customer complaints.

Strong:
Identify the top three verified causes
and attach supporting evidence for each.

Common mistakes

  • Activity confused with outcome.
  • Several agents own the final decision.
  • No completion rule.
  • Tasks outside the role domain.
Memory line

Assign agents outcomes they can verify, not vague activities.

Simple meaning

Boundaries define the agent’s domain, tools, data, permissions, decisions, time, and cost limits.

Weak prompt

Use any available method to complete the task.

Strong prompt
Use only approved read-only inventory tools.

Do not:
- Modify stock
- Contact suppliers
- Approve purchase orders
- Access customer payment data
Developer example
{
  "agent":"inventory_agent",
  "allowed_tools":[
    "get_inventory_history",
    "get_stockout_events"
  ],
  "forbidden_tools":[
    "update_inventory",
    "create_purchase_order"
  ]
}

Common mistakes

  • Tool access wider than the role.
  • Sensitive data shared unnecessarily.
  • Boundaries described only in prompts.
  • No deterministic enforcement.
Memory line

Role boundaries should be enforced by code, not trusted to prompts alone.

Simple meaning

Role inputs define the information an agent requires before starting.

Weak input

Analyse the issue.

Strong input
{
  "task_id":"T2",
  "region":"North",
  "period":"2026-Q2",
  "metric":"lost_sales",
  "known_facts":{
    "sales_decline":0.18
  }
}

Input should specify

  • Task ID
  • Goal and scope
  • Known facts
  • Data references
  • Constraints
  • Deadline
  • Required confidence

Common mistakes

  • Missing scope or date range.
  • Stale data passed as current.
  • Agent starts with incomplete input.
  • No source information.
Mini exercise

An analysis task has no date range. What should happen?

Answer: Request clarification or mark it blocked.

Memory line

Clear input prevents agents from solving the wrong version of the task.

Simple meaning

Role outputs define exactly what an agent must return.

Weak output

Inventory seems to be a problem.

Strong output
{
  "task_id":"T2",
  "status":"complete",
  "finding":"North-region stockouts contributed significantly to lost sales",
  "evidence":[
    {"metric":"stockout_rate","value":0.31},
    {"metric":"correlation_with_lost_sales","value":0.82}
  ],
  "confidence":0.91,
  "open_questions":[]
}

Common mistakes

  • Free-form output only.
  • No confidence.
  • No evidence.
  • No task status.
  • Output does not match receiver needs.
Memory line

Good collaboration begins with outputs another agent can validate and use directly.

Simple meaning

Completion criteria define when an agent’s task is finished.

Weak rule

Stop when the analysis looks complete.

Strong rule
Task is complete when:
- Required data sources were checked
- At least one supported finding exists
- Evidence is attached
- Confidence is stated
- Open questions are recorded
Example

If two required documents are unavailable, the correct status is blocked, not complete.

Common mistakes

  • Complete with missing evidence.
  • Completion based on output length.
  • Blocked and failed treated as complete.
  • No common status vocabulary.
Memory line

Every role needs an objective definition of done.

Simple meaning

A shared status model helps every agent understand task progress.

pending
assigned
in_progress
blocked
waiting_for_input
waiting_for_approval
complete
failed
cancelled
Weak design

done = true/false

Strong design
{
  "task_id":"T4",
  "status":"blocked",
  "blocker":"pricing data unavailable",
  "required_action":"request_data_access"
}

Common mistakes

  • Complete used for partial work.
  • No blocked reason.
  • No owner.
  • Status changes not audited.
Memory line

Rich task status prevents coordinators from guessing what happened.

Simple meaning

A handoff transfers responsibility for a task from one agent to another.

Handoff = Context Transfer + Responsibility Transfer
Weak handoff

Please take a look at this.

Strong handoff
{
  "message_type":"HANDOFF",
  "task_id":"T4",
  "from_agent":"research_agent",
  "to_agent":"analysis_agent",
  "goal":"Rank verified causes of sales decline",
  "completed_work":[
    "inventory evidence collected",
    "pricing evidence collected"
  ],
  "required_output":{
    "ranked_causes":"array",
    "confidence":"number"
  },
  "constraints":[
    "Use only attached evidence"
  ]
}

Common mistakes

  • Ownership remains ambiguous.
  • Receiver does not acknowledge.
  • Context omitted.
  • No expected output.
  • Both agents remain active owners.
Memory line

A handoff transfers task ownership, not just a message.

Simple meaning

A reliable handoff follows explicit stages.

Prepare→Validate→Send→Acknowledge→Transfer owner→Execute
Weak design

Send a message and assume the other agent owns it.

Strong design

Ownership changes only after receiver acknowledgment and shared-state update.

Common mistakes

  • Ownership updated before acceptance.
  • Target agent unavailable.
  • Required context missing.
  • No acknowledgment timeout.
Memory line

A handoff is complete only when the receiver accepts responsibility.

Simple meaning

Every handoff should be checked before processing.

Validate

  • Sender and receiver identity
  • Task ID and current owner
  • Required capability
  • Required permission
  • Input and output schema
  • Context completeness
  • Task version and deadline
Weak design

Accept every handoff message.

Strong design

Reject when capability, permissions, required fields, ownership, or task state do not match.

Example
Research Agent hands off payment execution
to Analysis Agent.

Correct result:
Reject handoff: capability mismatch

Common mistakes

  • Receiver chosen by name similarity.
  • Permission not checked.
  • Duplicate handoff accepted.
  • Stale task version used.
Memory line
