Handoffs should be validated as strictly as tool calls.

Simple meaning

Context tells the receiver what has happened and what remains.

Goal
Current state
Completed work
Verified facts
Rejected hypotheses
Open questions
Constraints
Expected output
Weak context

Continue the analysis.

Strong context
Verified:
- North stockout rate = 31%
- Lost-sales correlation = 0.82

Rejected:
- Supplier delay as primary cause

Open:
- Campaign impact

Required:
Rank verified causes using attached evidence

Common mistakes

  • Entire conversation copied.
  • Important conclusions omitted.
  • Assumptions presented as facts.
  • Sensitive data included unnecessarily.
Memory line

Transfer the minimum complete context, not the entire conversation.

Simple meaning

The receiver should explicitly confirm whether it can accept the handoff.

ACCEPTED
REJECTED
CLARIFICATION_REQUIRED
BLOCKED
Accepted
{
  "message_type":"HANDOFF_ACK",
  "task_id":"T4",
  "status":"ACCEPTED",
  "receiver":"analysis_agent",
  "missing_inputs":[]
}
Clarification required
{
  "message_type":"HANDOFF_ACK",
  "task_id":"T4",
  "status":"CLARIFICATION_REQUIRED",
  "missing_inputs":[
    "campaign performance data"
  ]
}

Common mistakes

  • Silent acceptance.
  • Work starts with missing inputs.
  • No acknowledgment timeout.
  • Coordinator ignores rejection.
Memory line

A receiver should confirm readiness before responsibility changes.

Simple meaning

Agents can collaborate through different orchestration patterns.

PatternBest use
RouterSend request to one specialist
SupervisorSelect one worker at a time
Planner–workerSeparate planning and execution
PipelineSequential specialist stages
Parallel specialistsIndependent analysis
ReviewerIndependent quality check
Peer collaborationControlled negotiation
BlackboardShared evidence workspace
Weak design

Let all agents talk to each other.

Strong design

Choose one collaboration pattern based on dependencies, authority, and task structure.

Common mistake

Mixing several patterns without clear control.

Memory line

Collaboration structure should follow task structure.

Simple meaning

A pipeline passes work through agents in a fixed sequence.

Research→Analysis→Review→Report

When to use

  • Each stage depends on the previous one.
  • Responsibilities are sequential.
  • Output formats are stable.
  • Auditability matters.
Weak design

Let every agent start immediately.

Strong design

Start each agent only when the previous stage has produced a validated output.

Common mistakes

  • Partial output passed downstream.
  • No schema validation between stages.
  • Failure restarts everything.
  • No correction return path.
Memory line

Pipelines are reliable when each stage produces a validated contract for the next.

Simple meaning

Independent agents perform separate tasks at the same time.

Example
Inventory Agent ─┐
Pricing Agent   ──┼→ Review Agent
Campaign Agent  ──┘

When to use

  • Tasks are independent.
  • Inputs are ready.
  • Outputs can be merged.
  • No conflicting writes occur.
Weak design

Run every agent at once.

Strong design

Run only independent read-only tasks in parallel and synchronize before decisions or write actions.

Common mistakes

  • Dependent tasks parallelized.
  • Same state fields updated.
  • Duplicate data retrieval.
  • No merge rule.
Mini exercise

Can policy retrieval and final refund execution run in parallel?

Answer: No.

Memory line

Parallelism is safe only when tasks do not depend on or conflict with each other.

Simple meaning

A reviewer agent evaluates another agent’s result against evidence and criteria.

Reviewer responsibilities

  • Check evidence
  • Check completeness
  • Find contradictions
  • Verify constraints
  • Reject unsupported claims
  • Request correction
Weak reviewer prompt

Find something wrong with the result.

Strong reviewer prompt

Evaluate the result against the goal, evidence, output schema, constraints, and success criteria. Return only material defects.

Example
Analyst:
Pricing caused the decline.

Reviewer:
Evidence supports only a minor pricing impact.
The conclusion overstates the finding.

Common mistakes

  • Reviewer forced to disagree.
  • No evidence access.
  • Reviewer executes corrections directly.
  • Endless review loops.
Memory line

A reviewer validates work; it should not manufacture disagreement.

Simple meaning

Peer agents exchange information directly to refine a result.

Weak design

Discuss until all agents agree.

Strong design

Allow at most two structured exchanges. Each message must contain new evidence, a specific question, or a proposed update.

Example
Inventory Agent:
Stockouts affected the North region.

Campaign Agent:
Traffic declined nationally.

Reviewer:
Both factors contributed at different scopes.

Common mistakes

  • Endless conversation.
  • Same evidence repeated.
  • No ownership.
  • Consensus treated as truth.
Memory line

Peer collaboration should add evidence or change state; otherwise it should stop.

Simple meaning

Agents contribute findings to a shared workspace rather than messaging each other continuously.

Shared workspace
{
  "facts":[],
  "hypotheses":[],
  "evidence":[],
  "open_questions":[],
  "recommendations":[]
}

When to use

  • Several agents contribute independently.
  • A shared evidence base is useful.
  • Work happens asynchronously.
  • Direct conversation adds overhead.

Common mistakes

  • No ownership of entries.
  • Unverified findings mixed with facts.
  • Agents overwrite each other.
  • No version history.
Memory line

A shared workspace can coordinate agents without making them converse continuously.

Simple meaning

Conflict occurs when agents disagree about facts, plans, permissions, ownership, or priority.

Weak resolution

Choose the most confident agent.

Strong resolution

Compare source authority, evidence freshness, completeness, scope, applicable policy, and task ownership. Escalate unresolved material conflicts.

Example
Agent A:
Order is delivered.

Agent B:
Order is in transit.

Resolution:
Query authoritative courier system
and use the freshest timestamp.

Common mistakes

  • Majority vote.
  • Highest confidence wins.
  • Conflict hidden.
  • Both outputs stored as facts.
Memory line

Resolve conflict with stronger evidence, not stronger language.

Simple meaning

An agent may hand off work only to a role already authorised to perform it.

Unsafe pattern
Finance Agent cannot execute payment
→ asks another agent to execute it
Safe pattern
Finance Agent prepares recommendation
→ Approval layer validates
→ Authorized workflow executes
Strong rule

An agent may delegate a task only to a role explicitly authorized for that task.

Common mistakes

  • Permission checked only for sender.
  • Receiver inherits sender authority.
  • Handoff used to escalate privileges.
  • Shared credentials across roles.
Memory line

Delegation transfers work, not permission.

Simple meaning

A collaboration loop occurs when agents repeatedly hand work back and forth without progress.

Warning pattern
Agent A → Agent B
Agent B → Agent A
No new evidence
No state change
No completion

Controls

  • Maximum handoff count
  • Maximum exchange count
  • One current owner
  • Duplicate-message detection
  • Required state change
  • Time and cost budgets
Weak prompt

Keep collaborating until everyone agrees.

Strong prompt

Allow at most two exchanges. Continue only when new evidence appears, state changes, or a blocker is removed.

Mini exercise

Two agents exchange the same conclusion three times. What should happen?

Answer: Stop and escalate or finalize.

Memory line

Collaboration without state change is only conversation overhead.

Simple meaning

Observability records who owned each task and how responsibility changed.

What to record

  • Task ID
  • Current and previous owner
  • Handoff time and reason
  • Receiver acknowledgment
  • Inputs and outputs
  • State changes
  • Validation results
  • Cost and latency
Weak logging

Agents collaborated on the task.

Strong logging
Task: T4
Previous owner: Research Agent
New owner: Analysis Agent
Handoff reason: Evidence collection complete
Acknowledged: Yes
Expected output: Ranked causes
Deadline: 14:30

Common mistakes

  • Ownership changes not logged.
  • No correlation ID.
  • Agent identity missing.
  • Only final output stored.
Memory line

Every handoff should leave an auditable ownership trail.

Coordinator creates tasks
T1 Inventory analysis
T2 Pricing analysis
T3 Campaign analysis
T4 Rank causes
T5 Prepare recommendations
T6 Final review
Parallel specialists
Inventory Agent:
Stockouts strongly affected North region.

Pricing Agent:
Price increase had minor national impact.

Campaign Agent:
Traffic declined by 35%.
Structured handoff to reviewer
{
  "task_id":"T4",
  "from":"coordinator",
  "to":"reviewer_agent",
  "goal":"Rank causes using verified evidence",
  "inputs":[
    "inventory_result",
    "pricing_result",
    "campaign_result"
  ]
}
Reviewer output
1. Stockouts
2. Campaign traffic decline
3. Pricing impact
Recommendation handoff
{
  "task_id":"T5",
  "to":"recommendation_agent",
  "ranked_causes":[
    "stockouts",
    "campaign_decline",
    "pricing"
  ],
  "expected_output":"prioritized corrective actions"
}
Completion check
All tasks complete: Yes
Evidence attached: Yes
Conflicts resolved: Yes
Final review complete: Yes
Goal complete
User Goal
    ↓
Coordinator
    ↓
Task Definition
    ↓
Role and Capability Check
    ↓
Agent Assignment
    ↓
Agent Execution
    ↓
Structured Result
    ↓
Result Validation
    ↓
Handoff Required?
  ├── No → Complete task
  └── Yes
        ↓
   Handoff Validator
        ↓
   Receiver Acknowledgment
        ↓
   Ownership Update
        ↓
   Next Agent
task = coordinator.create_task(goal)

agent = registry.select(
    capability=task.required_capability,
    permissions=task.required_permissions
)

assign(task, agent)

result = agent.execute(task)
validate_result(result)

if task.requires_handoff:
    handoff = build_handoff(
        task=task,
        result=result,
        target_agent=task.next_owner
    )

    validate_handoff(handoff)
    acknowledgment = send_handoff(handoff)

    if acknowledgment.status == "ACCEPTED":
        update_owner(
            task_id=task.id,
            new_owner=handoff.to_agent
        )
    else:
        coordinator.handle_rejection(
            task,
            acknowledgment
        )
AreaQuestion
RoleIs the responsibility specific?
OwnershipIs there one current owner?
BoundaryAre allowed and forbidden actions clear?
ToolsAre tools scoped to the role?
InputDoes the agent have complete required input?
OutputIs the output schema defined?
CompletionIs definition of done explicit?
StatusAre blocked and failed states supported?
HandoffDoes it transfer responsibility clearly?
ValidationIs the receiver capable and authorised?
ContextIs the minimum complete context included?
AcknowledgmentDid the receiver accept ownership?
CollaborationIs the pattern suitable?
ConflictIs there an evidence-based resolution path?
PermissionsCan delegation bypass authority?
LoopsAre exchange and handoff limits defined?
ObservabilityCan ownership changes be traced?
StopWho decides the goal is complete?
Define Role → Assign Task → Produce Structured Output → Validate Handoff → Transfer Ownership → Verify Completion
RoleClear responsibility and boundaries.
ContractStructured input and output.
HandoffValidated ownership transfer.
CollaborationLimited, traceable, evidence-based.
Final memory line

One clear owner, structured handoffs, limited communication, and evidence-based validation create reliable collaboration.

Quick knowledge check

1. What is an agent role?
A defined responsibility with explicit inputs, outputs, tools, permissions, and boundaries.
2. Why are role boundaries important?
They prevent agents from acting outside their authority.
3. What is a handoff?
A structured transfer of task context and responsibility.
4. When is a handoff complete?
When the receiver accepts and ownership is updated.
5. What should a handoff contain?
Task ID, goal, state, completed work, inputs, expected output, constraints, and owner details.
6. Why use structured outputs?
They make validation and downstream processing reliable.
7. What is pipeline collaboration?
Agents work in a fixed sequential chain.
8. What is parallel collaboration?
Independent agents work simultaneously and outputs are merged later.
9. Can delegation transfer permission?
No. The receiver must already be authorised.
10. Safest principle?
One clear owner, structured handoffs, limited communication, and evidence-based validation.

Reliable orchestration separates destination selection, goal ownership, task decomposition, and bounded execution.

Use the simplest coordination structure that can reliably complete the task.
RouterSupervisorPlannerWorker

Simple meaning

Large agentic tasks often need separate coordination responsibilities.

RoleMain responsibility
RouterSelects the correct destination
SupervisorControls overall execution
PlannerDecomposes the goal into tasks
WorkerExecutes a bounded task
Business example
User asks:
Investigate why North-region sales declined.

Router:
Selects Sales Investigation system

Supervisor:
Owns the overall goal

Planner:
Creates inventory, pricing, and campaign tasks

Workers:
Perform each analysis

Common mistake

Creating four model agents when one controller and two workers would be enough.

Memory line

Router chooses the destination, supervisor owns the goal, planner defines the work, and workers execute it.

Simple meaning

A router decides whether a request should go to a workflow, specialist agent, tool, human queue, or clarification path.

Weak prompt

Choose any agent that seems relevant.

Strong prompt

Classify the request into one approved destination and return destination, confidence, reason, and clarification_required.

Example
{
  "destination":"logistics_support_agent",
  "confidence":0.96,
  "reason":"Request concerns shipment delivery",
  "clarification_required":false
}

When to use

  • Requests map to clear domains.
  • One main destination is enough.
  • Low coordination overhead is desired.

When not to use

  • Several agents must collaborate.
  • The route depends on investigation.
  • Multiple intents are present.

Common mistakes

  • Invented destinations.
  • No confidence threshold.
  • No fallback route.
  • Sensitive route not validated.
Mini exercise

“Review this contract and explain the payment clause.” Which destination?

Answer: A legal or contract-analysis agent.

Memory line

A router chooses where work starts; it does not manage the whole task.

Deterministic routerModel router
Uses fixed conditionsUses semantic interpretation
Highly predictableMore flexible
Easy to testNeeds evaluation
Best for structured inputsBest for ambiguous language
Lower costHigher cost
Deterministic example
if request_type == "refund_status":
    destination = "refund_status_workflow"
Strong design

Use deterministic routing when structured fields provide the answer. Use model routing only when language interpretation is required.

Common mistake

Calling an LLM when the API already contains request_type="refund_status".

Memory line

Use rules when the route is known and models when meaning must be interpreted.

Simple meaning

The router should not force a destination when confidence is too low.

Weak design

Always choose one agent.

Strong design

Below threshold, ask for clarification, send to triage, escalate to a human, or use a safe general agent.

Example
{
  "destination":null,
  "confidence":0.42,
  "clarification_required":true,
  "question":"Is your issue about a refund, shipment, or account payment?"
}

Common mistakes

  • Confidence not calibrated.
  • Low confidence ignored.
  • No safe fallback.
  • Keyword-only routing.
Memory line

A router should know when it does not know the destination.

Simple meaning

A supervisor owns the overall goal and controls which component acts next.

Weak prompt

Manage the other agents.

Strong prompt

Read shared state, identify the next ready task, select one authorised worker, validate the result, update progress, and continue, reassign, escalate, or stop.

Example
Goal:
Investigate sales decline

Current state:
Inventory complete
Pricing complete
Campaign pending

Supervisor decision:
Assign Campaign Agent

Common mistakes

  • Supervisor performs all specialist work.
  • No shared-state check.
  • Worker outputs trusted automatically.
  • No completion criteria.
Memory line

The supervisor controls progress; it should not become every specialist.

Simple meaning

The supervisor manages coordination rather than domain execution.

Maintain goal
Track tasks
Select ready work
Assign workers
Validate results
Handle failures
Enforce budgets
Decide completion
Supervisor state
{
  "goal":"Investigate sales decline",
  "status":"in_progress",
  "tasks":{
    "T1":{"owner":"inventory_agent","status":"complete"},
    "T2":{"owner":"pricing_agent","status":"complete"},
    "T3":{"owner":"campaign_agent","status":"pending"}
  }
}

Common mistake

Allowing the supervisor to operate without structured task state.

Memory line

A supervisor coordinates from shared state, not from memory alone.

User Goal→Supervisor→Worker→Supervisor→Continue or Stop

When to use

  • Central authority matters.
  • Work is mainly sequential.
  • Worker roles are distinct.
  • Strong auditability is required.

When not to use

It may become inefficient when many tasks are fully independent.

Developer example
while not goal_complete(state):
    task = supervisor.select_ready_task(state)
    worker = registry.select_worker(task)
    result = worker.execute(task)
    validated = validate_result(result)
    update_state(state, task, validated)

Common mistakes

  • Unnecessary worker calls.
  • No selection policy.
  • No timeout.
  • Worker rejection not handled.
Memory line

The supervisor pattern is centralised orchestration with specialist execution.

Simple meaning

A planner converts a goal into tasks, dependencies, required capabilities, expected outputs, and success criteria.

Weak prompt

Create a detailed plan.

Strong prompt

Decompose the goal into the minimum necessary tasks. For each task return task_id, objective, dependencies, required capability, expected output, and success criteria.

Example
[
  {
    "task_id":"T1",
    "objective":"Analyse inventory shortages",
    "dependencies":[],
    "required_capability":"inventory_analysis"
  },
  {
    "task_id":"T2",
    "objective":"Analyse pricing changes",
    "dependencies":[],
    "required_capability":"pricing_analysis"
  },
  {
    "task_id":"T3",
    "objective":"Rank verified causes",
    "dependencies":["T1","T2"],
    "required_capability":"evidence_review"
  }
]

Common mistakes

  • Too many tiny tasks.
  • No dependencies.
  • Overlapping tasks.
  • No completion criteria.
Mini exercise

Should “Write final recommendation” start before evidence tasks finish?

Answer: No.

Memory line

A planner defines executable work, not merely a list of ideas.

PlannerSupervisor
Creates task structureControls execution
Defines dependenciesTracks status
Suggests capability needsSelects actual workers
Produces planUpdates progress
Runs once or occasionallyRuns throughout execution
Example
Planner:
T1 Inventory
T2 Campaign
T3 Compare findings

Supervisor:
Run T1 and T2
Wait for completion
Then start T3

Common mistake

Combining planning and supervision without distinguishing responsibilities.

Memory line

The planner defines what should happen; the supervisor manages what is happening.

Simple meaning

Static planning creates most tasks at the beginning. Dynamic planning revises tasks as evidence appears.

Weak design

Follow the original plan regardless of new evidence.

Strong design

Replan only when a material observation changes required tasks, dependencies, risk, available tools, or goal feasibility.

Example
Initial plan:
Check courier
Retrieve replacement policy
Create replacement

Observation:
Order was never paid

Revised plan:
Cancel courier and replacement tasks
Start payment-recovery path
Memory line

Replanning should follow material evidence, not every minor detail.

Simple meaning

A worker performs one bounded assignment and returns a structured result.

Weak role

Worker: Help solve the main goal.

Strong role
Inventory Worker

Task:
Determine whether stockouts contributed to lost sales.

Allowed tools:
- get_inventory_history
- get_stockout_events

Expected output:
- finding
- evidence
- confidence
- task status

Forbidden:
- Modify stock
- Create recommendations outside inventory scope

Common mistakes

  • Worker changes the main goal.
  • Worker creates tasks without permission.
  • Worker returns free-form output only.
  • Worker acts outside its role.
Memory line

A worker should execute one bounded assignment and return a validated result.

Required fields

Task ID
Objective
Scope
Known facts
Allowed tools
Expected output
Success criteria
Escalation rule
Weak assignment

Check inventory.

Strong assignment
{
  "task_id":"T1",
  "objective":"Determine whether stockouts caused lost sales",
  "scope":{
    "region":"North",
    "period":"2026-Q2"
  },
  "allowed_tools":[
    "get_inventory_history",
    "get_lost_sales"
  ],
  "expected_output":{
    "finding":"string",
    "evidence":"array",
    "confidence":"number"
  }
}

Common mistake

Sending broad shared context instead of a task-specific contract.

Memory line

Workers perform better when the assignment is narrow, complete, and testable.

Example
{
  "task_id":"T1",
  "worker":"inventory_agent",
  "status":"complete",
  "finding":"Stockouts materially affected North-region sales",
  "evidence":[
    {"metric":"stockout_rate","value":0.31},
    {"metric":"lost_sales_correlation","value":0.82}
  ],
  "confidence":0.91,
  "open_questions":[]
}

Result statuses

complete
blocked
failed
clarification_required
approval_required

Common mistakes

  • No task ID.
  • No status.
  • No evidence.
  • No confidence.
  • Instructions for unrelated workers.
Memory line

A worker result should be easy to validate, store, and hand to the next component.

Selection factors

  • Required expertise
  • Available tools
  • Data permissions
  • Current workload
  • Model capability
  • Cost
  • Latency
  • Reliability history
Weak selection

Choose the agent whose name looks closest.

Strong selection

Select a worker only when capability matches, required tools exist, permission is sufficient, the worker is available, and cost/latency fit.

Developer example
worker = registry.select(
    capability=task.required_capability,
    permissions=task.required_permissions,
    max_cost=task.max_cost
)

Common mistakes

  • No permission check.
  • First available always chosen.
  • Reliability not measured.
  • No fallback worker.
Memory line

Worker selection should match capability, permission, and operating constraints.

Simple meaning

The agent registry stores approved agents, capabilities, tools, permissions, cost, reliability, and availability.

Example
[
  {
    "agent_id":"inventory_agent",
    "capabilities":[
      "inventory_analysis",
      "stockout_analysis"
    ],
    "tools":[
      "get_inventory_history",
      "get_lost_sales"
    ],
    "permissions":[
      "inventory_read"
    ],
    "status":"available"
  }
]
Weak design

Let the model invent an agent when needed.

Strong design

Select only agents registered in the approved registry.

Common mistakes

  • Registry not versioned.
  • Stale availability.
  • Vague capabilities.
  • Permissions omitted.
Memory line

The registry tells the system which agents actually exist and what they may do.

Planner creates tasks→Supervisor validates→Approved task graph
Weak design

Execute every task produced by the planner.

Strong design

Treat the plan as a proposal. Validate task necessity, dependencies, worker availability, permissions, cost, and risk before assignment.

Common mistakes

  • Planner creates unrestricted tasks.
  • Supervisor skips validation.
  • Duplicate tasks accepted.
  • High-risk task lacks approval gate.
Memory line

The planner proposes work; the supervisor authorizes and controls it.

Assign→Acknowledge→Execute→Return result→Validate→Update state
Weak assignment

Please investigate and report back.

Strong assignment
{
  "message_type":"TASK_ASSIGNMENT",
  "task_id":"T1",
  "worker":"inventory_agent",
  "objective":"Analyse North-region stockouts",
  "expected_output_schema":"inventory_finding_v1"
}

Common mistakes

  • No acknowledgment.
  • Incomplete input.
  • Result not validated.
  • Ownership ambiguous.
Memory line

Supervisor–worker communication should use structured assignments and results.

Simple meaning

The router selects the appropriate supervisor or system, then hands over the extracted goal and context.

Weak design

The router selects the destination and performs the full task.

Strong design

Router output should contain destination, goal, confidence, and extracted context. The supervisor owns execution after routing.

Example
User:
Investigate why sales declined

Router:
sales_investigation_supervisor

Supervisor:
creates and manages investigation tasks

Common mistakes

  • Context lost during routing.
  • Router retains ownership.
  • Supervisor receives only raw user text.
  • Destination not validated.
Memory line

Routing transfers the request; supervision owns the execution.

Example
Inventory Worker ─┐
Pricing Worker   ──┼→ Reviewer
Campaign Worker  ──┘

When to use

  • Tasks are independent.
  • Inputs are ready.
  • Outputs do not conflict.
  • Tool limits allow it.
Weak design

Start every planned task immediately.

Strong design

Start only tasks with all dependencies satisfied. Parallelize read-only independent work and synchronize before combined decisions.

Common mistakes

  • Dependent tasks started early.
  • Shared state conflicts.
  • Duplicate calls.
  • No merge step.
Memory line

Parallel workers reduce latency only when their work is truly independent.

Simple meaning

When a worker fails, the supervisor chooses whether to retry, correct input, assign a fallback worker, mark blocked, or escalate.

Weak design

Restart the entire agent system.

Strong design

Preserve completed tasks, recover only the failed assignment, and use retry limits and fallback selection.

Example
Pricing Worker:
Tool timeout

Supervisor:
Retry once
Then assign fallback Pricing Worker
Preserve Inventory and Campaign results

Common mistakes

  • All tasks restarted.
  • Successful outputs lost.
  • Permanent errors retried.
  • No fallback worker.
Memory line

Recover the smallest failed task, not the entire goal.

Warning signs

  • Too many worker messages
  • High supervisor token use
  • Slow task assignment
  • Large supervisor context
  • Independent work waiting unnecessarily

Solutions

  • Deterministic routing
  • Batch status updates
  • Bounded worker autonomy
  • Task queues
  • Parallel independent work
  • Summarised worker results
Weak design

Every worker asks before every read-only tool call.

Strong design

Workers may perform approved actions within task boundaries. Supervisor approval is needed only for scope changes, high-risk actions, new tasks, or escalation.

Memory line

Central control should not require central micromanagement.

Warning pattern
Supervisor → Worker A
Worker A → Supervisor
Supervisor → Worker B
Worker B → Supervisor
No new evidence
No state change

Controls

  • Maximum assignment count
  • Single task owner
  • Duplicate-task detection
  • State-change requirement
  • Escalation after repeated failure
  • Cost and time limits
Weak prompt

Keep assigning until someone solves it.

Strong prompt

After two failed assignments, stop automatic reassignment, summarise failure evidence, and escalate or request clarification.

Common mistakes

  • Same task renamed and reassigned.
  • Failure reason ignored.
  • No retry budget.
  • No final escalation path.
Memory line

Reassignment is useful only when the next attempt is meaningfully different.

What to record

RouterDestination, confidence, reason, fallback.
PlannerTasks, dependencies, revisions.
SupervisorAssignments, validation, stop decision.
WorkerTools, evidence, result, cost, duration.
Trace example
Route:
sales_investigation_supervisor

Plan:
T1 Inventory
T2 Pricing
T3 Campaign
T4 Review

Assignment:
T1 → Inventory Agent

Result:
Complete, confidence 0.91

State change:
T1 complete

Common mistakes

  • Only final answer logged.
  • No task or agent identity.
  • No revision history.
  • Cost not separated by role.
Memory line

Role-level tracing makes coordination failures visible.

Router
{
  "destination":"sales_investigation_supervisor",
  "confidence":0.97
}
Planner
T1 Analyse inventory
T2 Analyse pricing
T3 Analyse campaigns
T4 Rank causes
T5 Recommend actions
Parallel workers
Inventory:
Stockout rate = 31%
Correlation with lost sales = 0.82

Pricing:
Impact = low

Campaign:
Traffic decline = 35%
Reviewer
Ranked causes:
1. Stockouts
2. Campaign decline
3. Pricing
Recommendation worker
1. Rebalance North-region inventory
2. Restore high-performing campaigns
3. Test targeted price rollback
Completion check
Evidence validated: Yes
Causes ranked: Yes
Recommendations produced: Yes
Open critical tasks: No
Goal complete
User Request
    ↓
Router
    ↓
Selected Supervisor
    ↓
Planner
    ↓
Proposed Task Graph
    ↓
Supervisor Validation
    ↓
Agent Registry
    ↓
Worker Assignment
    ↓
Worker Execution
    ↓
Structured Result
    ↓
Result Validation
    ↓
Shared State Update
    ↓
Continue / Replan / Reassign / Stop
route = router.select_destination(request)
validate_route(route)

supervisor = registry.get(route.destination)

plan = planner.create_plan(
    goal=request.goal,
    constraints=request.constraints
)

validated_plan = supervisor.validate_plan(plan)

while not goal_complete(state):
    ready_tasks = get_ready_tasks(validated_plan, state)

    for task in ready_tasks:
        worker = registry.select(
            capability=task.capability,
            permissions=task.permissions
        )
        assign(task, worker)

    results = execute_assignments(ready_tasks)

    for result in results:
        validate_worker_result(result)
        update_state(state, result)

    if should_replan(state):
        validated_plan = supervisor.validate_plan(
            planner.revise_plan(state)
        )

    if should_escalate(state):
        break
AreaQuestion
RouterIs the destination from an approved list?
ConfidenceIs low-confidence routing handled safely?
SupervisorWho owns the overall goal?
PlannerAre tasks bounded and dependency-aware?
RegistryAre capabilities and permissions current?
WorkerIs the worker suitable?
InputIs the task contract complete?
OutputIs the result structured and evidence-based?
ValidationAre plans and results checked?
ParallelismAre tasks truly independent?
FailureCan one worker be replaced safely?
BottleneckIs the supervisor micromanaging?
LoopsAre reassignment limits defined?
StateIs progress stored centrally?
ObservabilityCan each role decision be traced?
StopWho verifies overall completion?
Route → Supervise → Plan → Assign → Execute → Validate
RouterChooses where work begins.
SupervisorOwns progress and completion.
PlannerDefines tasks and dependencies.
WorkerProduces bounded, verifiable results.
Final memory line

The router chooses where work begins, the supervisor owns progress, the planner defines the tasks, and workers produce bounded, verifiable results.

Quick knowledge check

1. What does a router do?
Selects the appropriate destination.
2. What does a supervisor do?
Owns the overall goal and controls execution progress.
3. What does a planner do?
Decomposes the goal into tasks, dependencies, and criteria.
4. What does a worker do?
Executes one bounded assignment and returns a structured result.
5. Planner versus supervisor?
The planner defines the work; the supervisor manages the work.
6. Why use an agent registry?
To select only approved agents with known capabilities and permissions.
7. When can workers run in parallel?
When tasks are independent and non-conflicting.
8. What happens when one worker fails?
Retry or replace only that task while preserving successful work.
9. How can supervisor bottlenecks be reduced?
Use deterministic routing, queues, bounded worker autonomy, and parallelism.
10. Safest coordination principle?
Clear roles, validated contracts, shared state, and one authority for completion.

Human-in-the-loop systems allow agents to automate routine work while pausing for review, clarification, correction, or approval when risk and authority require it.

Humans should retain judgment and authority at the steps where automation should not act alone.
Agent ProposesPolicy ChecksHuman DecidesSystem ValidatesWorkflow Executes

Simple meaning

A human-in-the-loop system pauses at selected points so a person can review, approve, correct, or continue the task.

Weak design

Let the agent do everything automatically.

Strong design

Allow automatic investigation and recommendation, but require human involvement when authority is missing, confidence is low, policy is ambiguous, or impact is high.

Business example
Agent:
Checks payment and policy
Prepares refund recommendation

Human:
Approves ₹25,000 refund

Workflow:
Executes approved refund
Memory line

Human-in-the-loop adds judgment and authority where automation should not act alone.

Common reasons

  • Unclear intent
  • Conflicting evidence
  • Missing authority
  • Legal or financial risk
  • Irreversible action
  • Unusual exception
Example
Agent confidence: 0.48

Possible meanings:
- Refund
- Replacement
- Delivery update

Correct action:
Ask for clarification
Memory line

Human involvement is useful when the system lacks certainty, authority, or acceptable risk.

Human reviewHuman approval
Checks qualityGrants authority
Suggests changesAllows execution
Common for draftsCommon for sensitive actions
May be optionalMay be mandatory
Example
Review:
Check customer-email clarity

Approval:
Authorize ₹25,000 refund

Common mistake

Treating review comments as permission to execute.

Memory line

Review improves quality; approval grants authority.

Agent proposes→Policy checks→Pause→Human decision→Validate
Weak design

Send an approval request and continue execution.

Strong design

Set status to waiting_for_approval and continue only after the approver, exact action, scope, amount, and expiry are verified.

Memory line

An approval request is not an approval.

Approval triggers

  • Financial value exceeds limit
  • Action is destructive or irreversible
  • Legal or compliance risk exists
  • Customer impact is high
  • Confidence is below threshold
  • Agent leaves normal scope
Example
Under ₹5,000:
Create refund draft

Over ₹10,000:
Manager approval

Over ₹50,000:
Senior approval

Common mistake

Keeping approval thresholds only inside prompts.

Memory line

Approval rules should be deterministic and risk-based.

Weak prompt

Guess the likely intent and continue.

Strong prompt

If required information is missing, ask one focused clarification question before selecting a high-impact action.

Example
User:
Cancel it.

Agent:
Do you want to cancel the order,
replacement request, or refund request?
Memory line

Clarification is safer than confident guessing.

Simple meaning

A human may correct agent state, interpretation, or output.

Example
Agent state:
Customer requested refund

Human correction:
Customer requested replacement

System response:
- Update state
- Cancel invalid refund task
- Replan remaining work
- Record correction source

Common mistakes

  • Correction not written to state.
  • Old plan continues.
  • No audit record.
  • Authoritative correction ignored.
Memory line

Human correction must update state, not merely add another message.

Common draft types

  • Emails
  • Reports
  • Contracts
  • Recommendations
  • Code changes
  • Financial proposals
Weak design

Agent generates and sends the email.

Strong design

Agent creates a draft. A human may approve, edit, reject, or request revision.

Memory line

Draft-first design provides automation without surrendering final control.

waiting_for_input
waiting_for_review
waiting_for_approval
changes_requested
approved
rejected
escalated
Example
{
  "task_id":"T8",
  "status":"waiting_for_approval",
  "requested_action":"refund",
  "amount":25000,
  "required_role":"finance_manager"
}

Common mistake

Using only pending and complete.

Memory line

Explicit human-intervention states prevent accidental continuation.

Include

  • Requested action
  • Reason and evidence
  • Impact and risk
  • Policy reference
  • Amount or scope
  • Alternatives
  • Approver role
Weak request

Please approve this.

Strong request
{
  "action":"create_refund",
  "order_id":"2056",
  "amount":25000,
  "reason":"package_lost",
  "evidence":[
    "courier_status=package_lost"
  ],
  "policy":"refund-policy-v4.2",
  "required_approver":"finance_manager"
}
Memory line

A human should know exactly what is being approved and why.

Validate

  • Approver identity and role
  • Exact action
  • Amount and scope
  • Expiry
  • Conditions
  • Approval status
Unsafe mismatch
Approved:
Refund ₹5,000

Proposed execution:
Refund ₹25,000

Correct result:
Reject approval mismatch
Memory line

Approval must match the exact action being executed.

Possible outcomes

approved
rejected
changes_requested
clarification_required
Example
Human:
Reject refund.
Offer replacement instead.

Agent:
- Record rejection
- Cancel refund task
- Create replacement path
- Revalidate policy

Common mistakes

  • Rejected action remains active.
  • Same proposal retried.
  • Reason ignored.
  • Alternative not revalidated.
Memory line

Rejection changes the plan; it should not merely pause the old one.

Weak design

Require approval for every tool call.

Strong design

Allow low-risk read-only work automatically. Require approval only for high-impact actions, low-confidence decisions, scope changes, or irreversible operations.

Example
No approval:
- Read order status
- Search policy
- Create draft

Approval required:
- Send payment
- Delete data
- Suspend account
- Execute large refund
Memory line

Human oversight should focus on risk, not routine activity.

Possible timeout actions

  • Wait
  • Send reminder
  • Escalate to another approver
  • Expire the request
  • Cancel the action
Weak design

Keep waiting forever.

Strong design

When the approval window expires, mark the request expired, do not execute, and escalate or notify according to policy.

Memory line

Silence should never be treated as approval.

Record

  • Why human input was required
  • What was requested
  • Who responded
  • Decision and timestamp
  • Changes requested
  • State update
  • Execution result
Trace example
Task: T8
Status: waiting_for_approval
Action: Refund ₹25,000
Approver: Finance Manager
Decision: Approved
Time: 14:32
Execution result: Success
Memory line

Human decisions should leave an auditable trail.

Investigation
Order dispatched
Courier reports package lost
Customer requests refund
Policy result
Refund amount: ₹25,000
Autonomous limit: ₹10,000
Approval required
Approval request
{
  "action":"refund",
  "order_id":"2056",
  "amount":25000,
  "reason":"package_lost",
  "required_approver":"finance_manager"
}
Validation
Approver authorised: Yes
Order matches: Yes
Amount matches: Yes
Approval valid: Yes
Completion
Refund verified: Yes
Customer notified: Yes
Goal complete
User Goal
    ↓
Agent Investigation
    ↓
Proposed Action
    ↓
Risk and Policy Check
    ↓
Human Required?
 ├── No → Controlled Execution
 └── Yes
       ↓
   Approval Request
       ↓
   Human Decision
       ↓
   Approval Validator
       ↓
   Execute / Replan / Stop
proposal = agent.propose_action(state)
decision = policy_engine.evaluate(proposal)

if decision.requires_human:
    approval = request_approval(
        proposal=proposal,
        required_role=decision.required_role
    )

    state.status = "waiting_for_approval"

    if approval.status == "approved":
        validate_approval(approval, proposal)
        execute(proposal)

    elif approval.status == "changes_requested":
        update_state(state, approval.changes)
        replan(state)

    else:
        stop_or_escalate(state)
else:
    execute(proposal)
AreaQuestion
NeedIs human involvement genuinely required?
RiskIs the action high-impact or irreversible?
ConfidenceIs confidence below threshold?
AuthorityDoes the agent have permission?
RequestIs the approval request complete?
ApproverIs the approver authorised?
MatchDoes approval match the exact action?
StateIs the task paused correctly?
TimeoutWhat happens if no one responds?
RejectionDoes rejection update the plan?
OverloadAre humans reviewing too many low-risk actions?
AuditIs the complete approval trail recorded?
Agent Proposes → Policy Checks → Human Reviews or Approves → System Validates → Workflow Executes
ReviewImproves quality.
ApprovalGrants authority.
ValidationConfirms exact scope.
AuditRecords the full trail.
Final memory line

Human-in-the-loop does not mean humans control every step. It means humans retain judgment and authority where automation should not act alone.

Quick knowledge check

1. What is human-in-the-loop?
Humans review, approve, correct, or continue selected agent actions.
2. Review versus approval?
Review checks quality; approval grants authority.
3. When should approval be required?
For high-risk, irreversible, low-confidence, or policy-controlled actions.
4. Is an approval request an approval?
No.
5. What happens while approval is pending?
The action remains blocked.
6. Why validate approval?
To confirm identity, authority, scope, amount, and expiry.
7. What follows rejection?
The plan changes or stops.
8. Why avoid approval overload?
Too many approvals slow the system and reduce useful automation.
9. Can silence mean approval?
No.
10. Safest principle?
Use humans where judgment, authority, or risk requires them.

A trustworthy agent should receive only the authority required for its current role, task, resource, and approval state.

Agent capability is not the same as agent permission.
Identify AgentCheck RoleValidate ScopeCheck ApprovalAudit

Simple meaning

Permissions control which data, tools, APIs, files, records, and actions an agent may use.

Weak design

The agent can use any tool it needs.

Strong design
Support Agent may:
- Read order status
- Read courier status
- Create a reply draft

Support Agent may not:
- Issue payments
- Delete orders
- Access payroll
- Change permissions
Memory line

Capability describes what an agent can do; permission defines what it is allowed to do.

Why it matters

Agents may misread intent, use stale context, choose the wrong tool, or follow malicious instructions. Permissions limit the damage.

Example
Agent error:
Misreads “review account” as “close account”

Permission control:
Agent may read the account
but cannot close it
Memory line

Permissions limit the damage that incorrect reasoning can cause.

Weak design
Support Agent:
Full CRM, payment, order,
inventory, email, and admin access
Strong design
Support Agent:
- Read customer record
- Read order
- Read policy
- Create response draft

Benefits

  • Smaller blast radius
  • Lower data exposure
  • Less prompt-injection impact
  • Easier auditing
Memory line

The safest permission is the one the agent does not need.

Permission typeExample
ReadView order status
CreateCreate support ticket
UpdateChange ticket priority
DeleteRemove a record
ExecuteTrigger payment
ApproveApprove refund
AdministerChange user permissions
ExportDownload customer data
Memory line

Read, write, approve, and administer should be treated as separate permissions.

Good uses

  • Investigation
  • Research
  • Policy lookup
  • Incident diagnosis
  • Analytics
  • Audit review
Example
Read-only tools:
- get_order
- get_payment_status
- get_courier_status
- search_policy

Common mistake

Giving write access because the agent may eventually recommend an action.

Memory line

Start agents with read-only access and expand only when proven necessary.

Examples

  • Create draft
  • Add internal note
  • Update ticket label
  • Save recommendation
  • Create pending request
Weak design

Agent sends the email directly.

Strong design

Agent creates an email draft; a human or workflow sends it later.

Memory line

Prefer draft, pending, and reversible actions before direct execution.

Examples

  • Send money
  • Delete production data
  • Suspend account
  • Terminate contract
  • Change access control
  • Publish externally
  • Deploy to production

Required controls

  • Strong validation
  • Explicit approval
  • Limited scope
  • Full audit trail
  • Reversibility where possible
Memory line

The greater the impact, the narrower the permission and stronger the control.

Support role
{
  "role":"support_agent",
  "permissions":[
    "order.read",
    "shipment.read",
    "policy.read",
    "reply_draft.create"
  ]
}
Finance role
{
  "role":"finance_agent",
  "permissions":[
    "payment.read",
    "refund_draft.create"
  ]
}

Common mistake

Sharing one global credential across every agent.

Memory line

Agents should inherit permissions from approved roles, not from convenience.

Example
{
  "agent":"support_agent",
  "task_id":"T2056",
  "permission":"order.read",
  "resource":"order:2056",
  "expires_at":"2026-07-11T15:00:00Z"
}

Common mistakes

  • Access applies to all records.
  • No expiry.
  • Scope is not tied to the task.
  • Permission remains after completion.
Memory line

The best permission is limited by role, resource, task, and time.

Use cases

  • Short investigation
  • One-time document access
  • Emergency support
  • Temporary approval
  • Limited production diagnosis
Weak design

Grant access and remove it manually later.

Strong design

Grant access for 30 minutes and revoke automatically.

Memory line

Temporary authority should expire without depending on human memory.

Validate

  • Agent identity
  • Role
  • Task
  • Tool permission
  • Resource scope
  • Action type
  • Approval requirement
  • Expiry and current policy
Weak design

The tool trusts the agent’s claim.

Strong design
authorize(
    agent_id=agent.id,
    action="refund.create",
    resource="order:2056",
    task_id="T2056"
)
Memory line

Permission must be checked at execution time, not assumed from the prompt.

Separation
Finance Agent:
Creates refund recommendation

Finance Manager:
Approves refund

Payment Workflow:
Executes approved refund
Recommend → Approve → Execute

Common mistake

Allowing one agent to recommend, approve, and execute the same high-impact action.

Memory line

Permission allows a capability; approval authorizes a specific action.

Example
Agent A:
Investigates

Agent B or Human:
Approves

Workflow:
Executes

Auditor:
Reviews

Why it matters

  • Reduces fraud risk
  • Prevents self-approval
  • Improves accountability
  • Exposes hidden errors
Memory line

Sensitive actions should require more than one independent control point.

Request access→Validate reason→Approve→Grant scoped access→Expire
Weak design

The agent grants itself the required permission.

Strong design

An external authorization service validates the request and grants temporary scoped access.

Common mistakes

  • Agent modifies its own role.
  • No expiry.
  • Scope too broad.
  • No audit record.
Memory line

An agent must never be able to grant itself more authority.

Unsafe pattern
Support Agent lacks payment access
→ asks Research Agent to issue payment
Safe pattern
Support Agent creates recommendation
→ Authorized finance workflow validates
→ Payment executes with proper approval
Memory line

Delegation transfers work, not authority.

Correct responses

  • Stop the action
  • Record denial
  • Explain missing permission
  • Request authorized escalation
  • Select an allowed alternative
Weak response

Try another tool that may bypass the restriction.

Strong response

Permission denied. Do not retry through another path. Request approved escalation.

Memory line

Permission denial is a control signal, not a problem to bypass.

Safer methods

  • Tool-side authentication
  • Short-lived tokens
  • Scoped credentials
  • Secret vaults
  • Service identities
  • Per-tool authorization
Weak design

Place the production API key in the prompt.

Strong design

The agent calls an approved tool; the tool authenticates outside model context.

Common mistakes

  • Secrets included in prompts.
  • Credentials logged.
  • One credential shared by all agents.
  • Long-lived unrestricted tokens.
Memory line

Models should request capabilities, not handle raw credentials.

Record

  • Agent identity and role
  • Requested action
  • Resource
  • Permission decision
  • Policy used
  • Approval reference
  • Temporary grant and expiry
  • Execution result
Trace example
Agent: finance_agent
Task: T2056
Action: refund.execute
Resource: order:2056
Permission: denied
Reason: manager approval missing
Memory line

Every sensitive permission decision should be traceable.

Support Agent permissions
order.read
payment.read
policy.read
refund_recommendation.create
Investigation
Order verified
Payment successful
Package lost
Refund allowed
Amount ₹25,000
Permission result
Proposed:
refund.execute

Support Agent:
Not authorised
Safe path
Create refund recommendation
→ Finance Manager approves
→ Payment workflow validates
→ Refund executes
Completion
Permission valid: Yes
Approval valid: Yes
Refund verified: Yes
Audit trail complete: Yes
Agent Request
    ↓
Identity Check
    ↓
Role Check
    ↓
Task and Resource Scope
    ↓
Permission Policy
    ↓
Approval Required?
 ├── No → Tool Execution
 └── Yes
       ↓
   Approval Validation
       ↓
   Scoped Execution
       ↓
   Audit Record
decision = authorize(
    agent_id=agent.id,
    role=agent.role,
    action=proposal.action,
    resource=proposal.resource,
    task_id=state.task_id
)

if decision.allowed is False:
    deny_and_record(decision)

elif decision.requires_approval:
    approval = get_valid_approval(proposal)

    if approval:
        execute_scoped(proposal)
    else:
        state.status = "waiting_for_approval"

else:
    execute_scoped(proposal)
AreaQuestion
RoleIs the role clearly defined?
NeedDoes the agent genuinely need this permission?
ScopeIs access limited to the correct resource?
ActionAre read, write, approve, and admin separated?
TimeDoes permission expire?
TaskIs permission tied to a task?
ApprovalIs high-impact execution blocked?
SeparationCan one agent investigate, approve, and execute?
DelegationCan a handoff bypass permissions?
CredentialsAre secrets outside model context?
DenialIs denied access handled safely?
AuditAre decisions traceable?
Identify Agent → Check Role → Validate Scope → Check Approval → Execute Minimally → Audit
Least privilegeMinimum required access.
ScopeRole, task, resource, and time.
ApprovalSpecific authorization for impact.
AuditTrace every sensitive decision.
Final memory line

A trustworthy agent is one that can do only what its current role, task, scope, and approval allow.

Quick knowledge check

1. What is least privilege?
Giving only the minimum access required.
2. Is capability the same as permission?
No.
3. Why start read-only?
It reduces operational risk.
4. What are reversible writes?
Drafts or pending actions that can be undone.
5. Should read, write, approve, and admin be separate?
Yes.
6. What is task-scoped permission?
Access limited to one task and resource.
7. Can an agent grant itself more access?
No.
8. Does delegation transfer authority?
No.
9. Where should credentials be handled?
Outside model context through secure tools or services.
10. Safest principle?
Grant the minimum authority required for the current task.

Retrieval-enabled agents identify missing facts, choose authoritative sources, validate evidence, store it in state, and reason from verified information.

Retrieve before reasoning when the answer depends on external, current, private, or authoritative information.
Identify NeedRetrieveValidateStore EvidenceReason and Act

Simple meaning

Retrieval allows an agent to fetch relevant information from documents, databases, APIs, vector stores, policies, tickets, files, or previous task records.

Weak design

Answer using only model knowledge.

Strong design

Before deciding, retrieve the relevant policy, customer record, and current order status. Use only verified evidence from approved sources.

Memory line

Retrieval gives the agent evidence beyond the model’s internal memory.

Simple meaning

RAG retrieves relevant information and supplies it to the model before generating a response or decision.

Query + Retrieved Context → Grounded Response
Simple RAG versus agentic RAG
Simple RAG:
User query → Retrieve → Generate

Agentic RAG:
Goal → Identify missing facts
→ Retrieve → Evaluate
→ Retrieve again if needed
→ Act or answer
Memory line

RAG provides evidence; the agent decides when and how to use it.

Agents need retrieval when information is

  • Current
  • Private
  • Domain-specific
  • Too large for the prompt
  • Frequently changing
  • Required for compliance
Example
Question:
Can Order 2056 receive a refund?

Retrieve:
- Order status
- Payment status
- Courier status
- Current refund policy
- Customer request
Memory line

Important decisions should use current evidence, not model memory alone.

Model memoryRetrieval
Learned during trainingFetched at runtime
May be outdatedCan be current
General knowledgeTask-specific knowledge
No private record accessCan access authorised data
Harder to verifyCan preserve evidence
Weak design

The policy is probably similar to standard refund rules.

Strong design

Retrieve the current approved refund policy and identify the exact applicable clause.

Memory line

Model memory provides understanding; retrieval provides task evidence.

SourceBest fit
Vector databasePolicy and documentation
SQL databaseExact records
APILive operational status
Search enginePublic information
File storeContracts and reports
CRM or ticket systemCustomer history
Method selection
Order ID lookup
→ Database or API

Policy meaning
→ Semantic search

Exact invoice number
→ Keyword or structured lookup
Memory line

Choose retrieval based on data structure, not because vector search is fashionable.

Structured retrievalBest for IDs, dates, amounts, status, and exact records.
Semantic retrievalBest for policies, procedures, documentation, and similar meaning.
Structured example
SELECT status
FROM orders
WHERE order_id = '2056';
Semantic example
When may a lost shipment
receive a refund?
Memory line

Use structured retrieval for exact facts and semantic retrieval for meaning.

Weak prompt

Search for useful information.

Strong prompt

Identify the minimum missing facts required for the task. For each fact, select the most authoritative source.

Example
Goal:
Resolve Order 2056

Missing facts:
- Order status
- Payment status
- Courier status
- Applicable policy
- Customer preference
Memory line

Good retrieval begins with a clear information need.

Weak query

refund

Strong query

Lost package refund eligibility for paid domestic orders under the current customer-support policy.

Structured query
{
  "order_id":"2056",
  "fields":[
    "status",
    "payment_status",
    "tracking_id"
  ]
}

Common mistakes

  • Query too broad
  • Identifiers omitted
  • Several questions mixed together
  • User text copied blindly
Memory line

A precise query improves evidence quality before reasoning begins.

Example
Question:
Why did North-region sales decline?

Retrieve separately:
- Sales trend
- Stockout history
- Pricing changes
- Campaign performance
- Regional events
Weak design

Run one broad search for everything.

Strong design

Create one focused retrieval task for each independent evidence category.

Memory line

Complex questions often require several focused retrievals, not one large query.

Useful filters

  • Region
  • Date
  • Customer
  • Document type
  • Version
  • Security level
  • Language
  • Product
Example
{
  "query":"lost shipment refund",
  "filters":{
    "document_type":"approved_policy",
    "region":"India",
    "effective_date":"current"
  }
}
Memory line

Metadata filters reduce irrelevant and unsafe retrieval.

Support Agent may retrieve
Order record
Shipment record
Support policy
Customer messages
Support Agent may not retrieve
Payroll
Employee health data
Unrelated customer records
Administrative credentials
Strong rule

Authorization must be checked before retrieval and before use.

Memory line

Retrieval access must follow the same least-privilege rules as tool execution.

Ranking factors

  • Relevance
  • Source authority
  • Freshness
  • Exact entity match
  • Version
  • Completeness
  • Permission
Weak design

Use the first search result.

Strong design

Prefer the most authoritative, current, entity-matched result.

Memory line

The nearest result is not always the best evidence.

Validate

  • Correct entity
  • Correct date
  • Correct policy version
  • Source authority
  • Completeness
  • Security classification
  • Freshness
Example
Retrieved:
Refund Policy v2.1
Expired in 2024

Current:
Refund Policy v4.2

Correct action:
Reject outdated evidence
Memory line

Retrieved content must be validated before entering agent state.

Conflict example
Order database:
Delivered

Courier API:
In transit

Customer:
Not received
Safe resolution
Compare timestamps
Check authoritative source
Retrieve delivery proof
Record unresolved conflict
Escalate if needed
Memory line

Conflicting retrieval should trigger verification, not confident guessing.

Weak response

The customer probably qualifies for a refund.

Strong response
The customer qualifies because:
- Payment is confirmed
- Courier marks parcel lost
- Policy v4.2 allows refund
Important distinction
Evidence:
What the source says

Inference:
What the agent concludes
Memory line

Grounded reasoning links every important conclusion to evidence.

A citation may include

  • Document ID
  • Section
  • Database record ID
  • API timestamp
  • Policy version
  • Source URL
  • Retrieval time
Example
{
  "finding":"Refund allowed",
  "evidence":[
    {
      "source":"refund-policy-v4.2",
      "section":"7.3",
      "retrieved_at":"2026-07-11T11:20:00Z"
    }
  ]
}
Memory line

Evidence without source references is difficult to verify or audit.

Identify missing fact→Retrieve→Validate→Update state→Check need
Weak design

Keep searching until many documents are found.

Strong design

Continue only when a required fact is missing, new evidence may resolve conflict, or confidence remains below threshold.

Stop when

  • Required facts are verified
  • No better source exists
  • Budget is exhausted
  • Human clarification is required
Memory line

Retrieval should stop when the evidence need is satisfied.

Common failures

  • No result
  • Irrelevant result
  • Outdated result
  • Permission denied
  • Source unavailable
  • Conflicting results
  • Invalid format
Weak response

No result found, so use model knowledge.

Strong response

No authoritative result found. Do not infer the policy. Mark blocked, reformulate, use an alternate source, or escalate.

Memory line

Missing evidence should reduce confidence, not increase invention.

Example state
{
  "known_facts":{
    "payment_status":{
      "value":"paid",
      "source":"payment_api",
      "verified":true
    },
    "courier_status":{
      "value":"package_lost",
      "source":"courier_api",
      "verified":true
    }
  }
}

Common mistake

Storing retrieved text without source, timestamp, or verification status.

Memory line

Store evidence as structured facts with source and verification metadata.

Missing facts
Order status
Payment status
Courier status
Refund policy
Customer preference
Exact retrieval
Order API: dispatched
Payment API: paid
Courier API: package_lost
Semantic retrieval
Query:
Lost package refund eligibility

Result:
Refund Policy v4.2, Section 7.3
Reasoning
Package is lost
Payment is confirmed
Current policy permits refund
Action
Create refund recommendation
Request approval if threshold exceeded
Completion
Evidence verified: Yes
Decision grounded: Yes
Source references stored: Yes
Agent Goal
    ↓
Information Need
    ↓
Retrieval Planner
    ↓
Source Selection
    ↓
Permission Check
    ↓
Query Execution
    ↓
Ranking and Filtering
    ↓
Evidence Validation
    ↓
State Update
    ↓
Reason / Retrieve Again / Stop
missing_facts = agent.identify_missing_facts(
    goal=state.goal,
    state=state
)

for fact in missing_facts:
    source = source_registry.select(
        fact=fact,
        permissions=agent.permissions
    )

    query = build_query(fact, state)
    results = source.retrieve(query)

    ranked = rank_results(
        results,
        authority=True,
        freshness=True,
        entity_match=True
    )

    evidence = validate_evidence(ranked)
    update_state_with_evidence(
        state,
        fact,
        evidence
    )

if evidence_sufficient(state):
    decision = agent.reason(state)
else:
    stop_or_escalate(state)
AreaQuestion
NeedWhat exact information is missing?
SourceWhich source is authoritative?
MethodStructured or semantic retrieval?
QueryIs the query precise?
FiltersAre entity, date, region, and version applied?
PermissionMay this agent access the source?
RankingAre authority and freshness considered?
ValidationIs the result current and correct?
ConflictHow are disagreements resolved?
CitationIs the source reference preserved?
StateIs evidence stored structurally?
LoopIs there a retrieval stop condition?
FailureWhat happens when no evidence is found?
ActionIs the decision grounded?
Identify Missing Fact → Retrieve → Validate → Store Evidence → Reason → Act
NeedRetrieve only what is missing.
SourcePrefer authority and freshness.
EvidenceValidate and preserve citations.
ActionReason only from verified facts.
Final memory line

Retrieval does not make an agent automatically correct. It provides evidence that must still be filtered, validated, cited, and used carefully.

Quick knowledge check

1. What is retrieval inside an agent?
Fetching task-relevant information from external sources.
2. What is RAG?
Retrieval-Augmented Generation.
3. How is agentic RAG different?
The agent decides when, what, and how often to retrieve.
4. When use structured retrieval?
For exact facts such as IDs, amounts, dates, and status.
5. When use semantic retrieval?
For policies, procedures, documentation, and meaning-based search.
6. Why use metadata filters?
To limit retrieval to the correct scope and version.
7. Should retrieved content be trusted automatically?
No. It must be validated.
8. What happens when evidence conflicts?
Verify authority, freshness, scope, and timestamps.
9. When should retrieval stop?
When required evidence is verified or safe progress is impossible.
10. Safest principle?
Retrieve authoritative evidence, validate it, preserve its source, and reason only from verified facts.

Reliable agent systems exchange typed, validated, authorized, versioned, and traceable messages instead of relying on vague prose.

Treat every agent message like an API contract.
Create MessageValidate SchemaAuthenticateCheck PermissionProcess Safely

Simple meaning

A structured output follows a defined format such as JSON, a typed object, or a table.

Weak output

The order seems delayed and probably needs replacement.

Strong output
{
  "task_id":"T2056",
  "status":"complete",
  "finding":"package_lost",
  "recommended_action":"create_replacement",
  "confidence":0.94
}

Why it matters

  • Easier to validate
  • Easier to store
  • Easier to route
  • Easier to audit
  • Easier to pass to tools or other agents
Memory line

Free text is easy to read; structured output is easier to automate and trust.

Common message purposes

  • Task assignments
  • Results
  • Clarification requests
  • Approval requests
  • Handoffs
  • Errors
  • Status updates
Weak design

Please check this and continue.

Strong design
{
  "message_type":"TASK_ASSIGNMENT",
  "task_id":"T4",
  "objective":"Rank verified causes of sales decline",
  "expected_output_schema":"ranked_causes_v1"
}
Memory line

Structured communication reduces ambiguity between agents.

Simple meaning

A schema defines what a message must contain and which values are valid.

Example schema
{
  "task_id":"string",
  "status":"complete | blocked | failed",
  "finding":"string",
  "evidence":"array",
  "confidence":"number between 0 and 1"
}

A schema defines

  • Required fields
  • Optional fields
  • Data types
  • Allowed values
  • Validation rules
Memory line

A schema is the agreement between sender and receiver.

Example
{
  "task_id":"T4",
  "status":"blocked",
  "finding":null,
  "blocker":"campaign data unavailable",
  "confidence":null
}

Here, task_id and status are always required. blocker becomes required when status is blocked.

Common mistake

Making every field optional.

Memory line

Required fields protect the workflow from incomplete messages.

TASK_ASSIGNMENT
TASK_ACKNOWLEDGMENT
TASK_RESULT
CLARIFICATION_REQUEST
HANDOFF
APPROVAL_REQUEST
ERROR
BLOCKED
FINAL_RESULT
Example
{
  "message_type":"CLARIFICATION_REQUEST",
  "task_id":"T7",
  "question":"Which date range should be analysed?",
  "missing_field":"period"
}

Common mistake

Using one generic message type for every situation.

Memory line

Message type tells the receiver how the content should be processed.

pending
assigned
in_progress
blocked
waiting_for_input
waiting_for_approval
complete
failed
cancelled
Weak output

Almost done.

Strong output
{
  "task_id":"T3",
  "status":"blocked",
  "blocker":"pricing API unavailable"
}
Memory line

Status should be explicit enough for the coordinator to act without guessing.

Assignment example
{
  "message_type":"TASK_ASSIGNMENT",
  "task_id":"T1",
  "worker":"inventory_agent",
  "objective":"Determine whether stockouts caused lost sales",
  "scope":{
    "region":"North",
    "period":"2026-Q2"
  },
  "allowed_tools":[
    "get_inventory_history",
    "get_lost_sales"
  ],
  "expected_output_schema":"inventory_finding_v1"
}

Common mistakes

  • No task ID
  • No scope
  • No tool boundary
  • No expected output format
  • No completion criteria
Memory line

A structured assignment converts a broad goal into executable work.

Result example
{
  "message_type":"TASK_RESULT",
  "task_id":"T1",
  "status":"complete",
  "finding":"Stockouts materially affected North-region sales",
  "evidence":[
    {"metric":"stockout_rate","value":0.31},
    {"metric":"lost_sales_correlation","value":0.82}
  ],
  "confidence":0.91
}
Memory line

A result should be easy to validate, store, compare, and reuse.

Handoff example
{
  "message_type":"HANDOFF",
  "task_id":"T4",
  "from_agent":"research_agent",
  "to_agent":"analysis_agent",
  "goal":"Rank verified causes",
  "completed_work":[
    "inventory evidence collected",
    "pricing evidence collected"
  ],
  "expected_output_schema":"ranked_causes_v1"
}
Weak handoff

Continue from here.

Memory line

A handoff should transfer structured responsibility, not conversation history.

Weak error

Something went wrong.

Strong error
{
  "message_type":"ERROR",
  "task_id":"T3",
  "error_code":"TOOL_TIMEOUT",
  "retryable":true,
  "attempt":1,
  "recommended_action":"retry_once"
}

Useful fields

  • Error code
  • Retryable flag
  • Tool name
  • Attempt count
  • Safe next action
  • Escalation requirement
Memory line

Structured errors turn failure into controlled recovery.

Validate

  • Message type
  • Sender identity
  • Task ID
  • Schema version
  • Required fields
  • Data types
  • Allowed values
  • Permission
  • Current task status
  • Duplicate message ID
Weak design

Accept every JSON-looking response.

Strong design

Reject messages that fail schema, ownership, permission, version, or task-state checks.

Memory line

Valid JSON is not necessarily valid agent communication.

Schema-valid but wrong
{
  "status":"complete",
  "confidence":1.4
}
Another invalid value
{
  "action":"refund",
  "amount":-5000
}

Semantic checks

  • Value range
  • Business rules
  • Entity match
  • Status consistency
  • Permission
  • Evidence completeness
Memory line

A message can be structurally valid and still be operationally wrong.

Example
{
  "schema":"task_result",
  "schema_version":"2.0",
  "task_id":"T1",
  "status":"complete"
}

Why it matters

  • Backward compatibility
  • Controlled rollout
  • Migration
  • Debugging
  • Deprecation

Common mistake

Changing field names without updating consumers.

Memory line

Schema versioning prevents silent communication breakage.

Safe strategies

  • Add optional fields
  • Avoid changing existing meanings
  • Support multiple versions temporarily
  • Use adapters
  • Reject unsupported versions clearly
Example
Receiver supports:
task_result v1 and v2

Sender sends:
task_result v3

Result:
unsupported_schema_version
Memory line

Communication should fail clearly rather than silently misinterpret data.

Useful identifiers

  • Message ID
  • Task ID
  • Workflow ID
  • Agent ID
  • Parent message ID
  • Correlation ID
Example
{
  "message_id":"MSG-881",
  "workflow_id":"WF-2056",
  "task_id":"T4",
  "correlation_id":"ORDER-2056"
}
Memory line

IDs connect individual messages into one auditable workflow.

Duplicate risks

  • Duplicate refund
  • Duplicate ticket
  • Duplicate email
  • Duplicate state update
Strong design

Every message has a unique message_id. Before processing, check whether it was already handled.

Example
{
  "message_id":"MSG-881",
  "idempotency_key":"order2056-refund-v1"
}
Memory line

Communication should be idempotent wherever duplicate processing could cause harm.

Risks

  • Malicious instructions
  • Prompt injection
  • Unauthorized requests
  • Sensitive data
  • Forged sender identity

Safe controls

  • Authenticate sender
  • Authorize action
  • Validate schema
  • Sanitize text fields
  • Separate data from instructions
  • Reject unknown message types
Weak design

Treat every field from another agent as trusted instruction.

Memory line

Structured format improves control, but does not make content trustworthy automatically.

Record

  • Sender and receiver
  • Message type
  • Schema version
  • Task ID
  • Validation result
  • Processing result
  • State change
  • Error
  • Duration and cost
Trace example
Message: MSG-881
Type: TASK_RESULT
Sender: Inventory Agent
Task: T1
Schema: task_result_v2
Validation: Passed
State update: T1 complete
Memory line

Every important message should leave a trace of what was received and what changed.

Assignment
{
  "message_type":"TASK_ASSIGNMENT",
  "task_id":"T1",
  "worker":"inventory_agent",
  "objective":"Analyse North-region stockouts",
  "expected_output_schema":"inventory_finding_v1"
}
Acknowledgment
{
  "message_type":"TASK_ACKNOWLEDGMENT",
  "task_id":"T1",
  "status":"accepted"
}
Result
{
  "message_type":"TASK_RESULT",
  "task_id":"T1",
  "status":"complete",
  "finding":"Stockouts contributed materially",
  "confidence":0.91
}
Handoff
{
  "message_type":"HANDOFF",
  "task_id":"T4",
  "to_agent":"reviewer_agent",
  "inputs":[
    "inventory_result",
    "pricing_result",
    "campaign_result"
  ]
}
Final result
{
  "message_type":"FINAL_RESULT",
  "workflow_id":"WF-SALES-01",
  "status":"complete",
  "ranked_causes":[
    "stockouts",
    "campaign_decline",
    "pricing"
  ]
}
Agent Produces Message
        ↓
Schema Validation
        ↓
Sender Authentication
        ↓
Permission Check
        ↓
Semantic Validation
        ↓
Duplicate Check
        ↓
Process Message
        ↓
Update Shared State
        ↓
Audit Record
message = receive_message()

validate_schema(
    message,
    expected_version="2.0"
)

authenticate_sender(message.sender)

authorize_message(
    sender=message.sender,
    message_type=message.message_type,
    task_id=message.task_id
)

validate_semantics(message)

if already_processed(message.message_id):
    return "duplicate_ignored"

result = process_message(message)

update_state(result)
record_audit(message, result)
AreaQuestion
TypeIs the message type explicit?
SchemaIs the structure defined?
Required fieldsAre essential fields mandatory?
StatusAre allowed statuses standardized?
SenderIs sender identity verified?
PermissionMay the sender issue this message?
VersionIs the schema version supported?
SemanticsDo the values make operational sense?
IDsAre message, task, and workflow IDs present?
DuplicateCan repeated processing be handled safely?
SecurityAre text fields treated as untrusted data?
AuditIs the resulting state change traceable?
Create Typed Message → Validate Schema → Authenticate → Check Permission → Process → Update State
TypeExplicit message purpose.
SchemaRequired fields and values.
SecurityAuthenticate and authorize.
TraceIDs, state change, and audit.
Final memory line

Reliable agent communication is not better prose. It is validated contracts that the system can process safely.

Quick knowledge check

1. What is a structured output?
A response that follows a defined machine-readable format.
2. Why are schemas useful?
They define required fields, types, and allowed values.
3. Why use message types?
They tell the receiver how to process the message.
4. What is semantic validation?
Checking whether values make operational sense.
5. Why version schemas?
To manage changes without silently breaking consumers.
6. Why include IDs?
For tracing, ownership, and duplicate detection.
7. Can valid JSON still be invalid?
Yes. It may fail schema, permission, or business rules.
8. Why is idempotency important?
It prevents duplicate processing from causing duplicate actions.
9. Are structured messages automatically trustworthy?
No. Sender, permission, and content still require validation.
10. Safest principle?
Treat every message as typed, validated, authorized, and traceable.

Context engineering determines what the agent sees, how information is structured, which sources are trusted, and what is excluded from the current decision.

Give the agent the minimum complete, verified, and relevant context for the current decision.
SelectValidateStructurePrioritizeUpdate

Simple meaning

Context engineering is the design of the information supplied to an agent during execution.

Weak design

Pass the entire conversation and every available document.

Strong design

Provide the current goal, relevant state, verified evidence, allowed tools, constraints, and required output format.

Memory line

Context engineering is choosing the right information, not the maximum information.

Bad context may cause

  • Wrong assumptions
  • Forgotten constraints
  • Repeated work
  • Incorrect tool selection
  • Higher token cost
  • Security exposure
Example
Goal:
Resolve delayed Order 2056

Missing:
- Refund limit
- Customer preference
- Current courier status

Risk:
Invalid resolution
Memory line

Missing context produces incomplete reasoning; excessive context produces noisy reasoning.

ContextMemory
Visible in the current model callStored outside the call
TemporaryMay persist
Limited by context windowCan be much larger
Directly influences reasoningMust be retrieved first
Example
Long-term memory:
Customer prefers email

Current context:
Preference retrieved because
the agent is drafting a notification
Memory line

Memory stores information; context presents selected information to the model.

Instruction contextRole, behavior, safety rules.
Goal contextOutcome, scope, success criteria.
State contextFacts, tasks, current status.
Evidence contextRetrieved documents and records.
Tool contextTools, schemas, permissions, risk.
Memory contextSelected preferences and procedures.
Memory line

Different context layers support different reasoning needs.

Static contextRole, safety instructions, tool schemas, business rules, output format.
Dynamic contextTool results, evidence, progress, approval status, user clarification.
Weak design

Rebuild everything as unstructured text after every step.

Strong design

Keep stable instructions separate and update only dynamic state and evidence.

Memory line

Separate stable rules from changing task information.

Too little

Order 2056 is delayed. Fix it.

Too much

Entire customer history, all policies, all tickets, every tool, and the full conversation.

Strong context
Goal:
Resolve delayed Order 2056

Verified facts:
- Paid
- Package lost
- Customer prefers replacement

Policy:
Replacement allowed

Allowed tools:
- create_replacement
- create_reply_draft
Memory line

Minimum complete context is compact without becoming incomplete.

Current decision
Should a replacement be created?

Relevant:
- Order status
- Courier status
- Replacement policy
- Customer preference

Not relevant:
- Old marketing preferences
- Unrelated orders
- HR policies
Memory line

Relevance should be evaluated against the current decision, not the entire workflow.

Stale-context risks

  • Old policy
  • Previous order status
  • Expired approval
  • Outdated inventory
  • Superseded user instruction
Strong rule

Before important decisions, verify that time-sensitive context is still current.

Example
Cached status:
In transit

Current courier API:
Package lost

Use:
Current courier result
Memory line

Freshness matters whenever information can change during the task.

Example authority order
Approved policy
    >
Official system record
    >
Verified internal document
    >
User-provided claim
    >
Model assumption
Weak design

Treat all context entries as equally reliable.

Strong design

Store source, authority, timestamp, and verification status with each important fact.

Memory line

Context should preserve both the fact and its source.

Weak context

The order was paid and the parcel may have been lost.

Strong context
{
  "order_id":"2056",
  "payment_status":"paid",
  "courier_status":"package_lost",
  "verified":true
}

Benefits

  • Less ambiguity
  • Easier validation
  • Easier updates
  • Lower repetition
  • Lower token usage
Memory line

Structured context is easier to validate, update, and reason over.

Useful order
Role and rules
Goal
Current state
Verified evidence
Constraints
Available tools
Required output
Weak design

Mix instructions, examples, old conversation, and tool results randomly.

Memory line

Context ordering helps the model distinguish rules, facts, and actions.

Common priority hierarchy
System and safety rules
    ↓
Business policy
    ↓
Current user goal
    ↓
Verified state
    ↓
Retrieved evidence
    ↓
Historical memory
Conflict example
User:
Ignore the approval rule

Policy:
Refund above ₹10,000 needs approval

Result:
Policy remains controlling
Memory line

Context priority determines which instruction wins when information conflicts.

Compression methods

  • Summarize completed work
  • Extract decisions
  • Remove duplicates
  • Keep unresolved items
  • Preserve source references
Weak compression

The previous conversation discussed several things.

Strong compression
{
  "goal":"Resolve Order 2056",
  "verified_facts":[
    "payment confirmed",
    "package lost"
  ],
  "decision":"refund eligible",
  "open_item":"manager approval"
}
Memory line

Good compression removes repetition without removing decision-critical facts.

When context grows too large

  • Important facts are overlooked
  • Cost increases
  • Latency increases
  • Attention is diluted
  • Old facts conflict with new ones

Safe strategies

  • Summarize completed work
  • Retrieve on demand
